```
Graham Hobbs wrote:
> Pete,
>
> Your 'emphatic' mods didn't compile and when I jerked them around,
> didn't work.

Well, I asked you to let me know and you did... :-)

I'm sorry.

Without knowing the precise nature of the jerking, or what your compiler 
said about it, I can't help further.

> I would post what happened but is all getting a bit
> heavy. I will look at the program, pity its not documented.

It is written in procedural COBOL. Some people argue that is 
"self-documenting"...:-)

I managed to maintain it without real difficulty (once I got my head around 
the style).

>
> Might there be a cobdata executable somewhere that runs on a Windows
> XP box I could try?

Yes, there is. I said as much.

There are two executables:

1. The application wrapped in C#, as downloadable from cobol21. This is a 
complete, standalone application. You can present your record definition on 
the system clipboard or from a local sequential file, and the output is 
written to a sequential file in the local directory.

Have you tried this? (You will need a .NET platform installed, in order to 
run it. The install will get it for you if it doesn't find it.)

2. A COM .dll which can be called from any language which supports the 
Component Object Model. So far people have used it from Fujitsu COBOL, 
VB.Net and C++, all running on the XP platform. This has no user interface 
and expects the input to be in a  local sequential file. You must 
instantiate the COBSTRUCT object and invoke the COBDATA Method of it. As 
your COBOL apparently doesn't support OO this may be difficult for you. 
(It's not impossible but you have to go "round the houses...")

If you can't get the code to compile on your platform why not simply rebuild 
it in your own environment? It is only COBOL...and you have the source. If 
you extract the separate methods in the OO version you could put them 
together, either as nested programs, or even as PERFORMED routines under a 
main control loop. This way you can compile it step by step and get 
something that runs native on your platform. I'm suggesting you use the OO 
version for re-construction because it has had amendments and enhancements 
applied to it. (For example, I extended Robert's code to support up to 1000 
datanames instead of 250...)

>
> Am doing tjhis because it's critical for the software package I'm
> working on and my own version of same is/was giving me so much trouble
> with OCCURS and REDEFINES that I decided to try cobdata.

I understand. Parsing (correctly) and dealing with COBOL record definitions 
is not easy, largely because of REDEFINES and OCCURS, but sometimes group 
fields can be problematic also. COBDATA correctly does this, even on very 
complex definitions, and every time I have thought I had a case where it got 
it wrong, it was actually correct, PROVIDED the COBOL was correct... 
obviously, invalid REDEFINEs and OCCURS will not guarantee a correct result 
from COBDATA.

Since it was released I have come to trust this program and it is used in 
some of my applications every day. Yesterday I used the standalone version 
of it it on a COBOL definition with 500 datanames in it and 24 REDEFINES. It 
worked correctly.

I believe the code is good and Robert did a good job with a difficult task.


> If it's of
> any interest I planned to include it as part of my product when it
> goes to market.

That's fine and doesn't violate the Licence.

Good luck with your project.

Pete.

<unreferenced previous snipped>
```

- **References:** `<g6m3h5deqg28t44ksc1f11eah41eh4cc9u@4ax.com> <7neu4uF3kr7taU1@mid.individual.net> <qr76h5lmlvl9bafb519e3v756o58gaeon4@4ax.com> <7ngs6tF3m16tgU1@mid.individual.net> <dinbh5tb8i5rr9o9cda61ma3a5626t1e0d@4ax.com> <7notr2F3mph8kU1@mid.individual.net> <C%0Sm.317186$Jp1.264537@en-nntp-06.dc1.easynews.com>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:
> Pete, Graham, et al,
>  In my spare time, I might try and convert the existing code to an
> ANSI'85 conforming source prgoram. I don't think it uses anything
> that couldn't be done that way.
>
I'm quite sure it doesn't. I'd do it myself but Idon't have the time or, 
really, the inclination.

I was hoping to get another "freebie" up on cobol21, which a couple of 
people have requested through private mail, but I haven't had time to do 
that either. Enhancements and extensions to the PRIMA Migration Toolset are 
taking all my time at the moment.  Coincidentally, I added internal use of 
COBDATA to the Toolset yesterday. (It was needed to establish the size of 
certain records and the offset and length of their primary key fields, for a 
process called "bagging" which is needed when a very large COBOL definition 
would result in creation of a RDB table which exceeds the limit for the 
RDBMS being used.) I can confirm that the COBOL COM .DLL works perfectly 
from C#.

It is an added reference to a C# project and 2 lines of code (excluding 
comments) as follows:

// create an instance of the COBDATA COM component...
COBSTRUCT.COBSTRUCTClass objCobstruct = new COBSTRUCT.COBSTRUCTClass();
objCobstruct.COBDATA();  // invoke the COBDATA method of the COM Class


The equivalent action in (Fujitsu) OO COBOL would be as follows:

REPOSITORY.

       *COM as COM. *> Sets up the Fujitsu COM interface Class

WORKING-STORAGE.
01     COM-prog-ID   pic x(21) value "COBSTRUCT.COBSTRUCT.1".
01     objCobstruct  OBJECT REFERENCE COM.

PROCEDURE DIVISION.

*>  create an instance of the COBDATA COM component...
          invoke COM "CREATE-OBJECT"
                     USING COM-prog-ID
                      RETURNING objCobstruct
          end-invoke
*>  invoke the COBDATA method...
          invoke objCobstruct "COBDATA"
          end-invoke

(10 lines of code excluding comments and headers, scattered all over the 
program, and with no IntelliSense guidance from the Fujitsu IDE...)

You can start to understand why people don't write OO COBOL. (Once you 
understand what is going on it is much quicker and cleaner to write in a 
modern language.  Like I said before, you CAN do it in COBOL , but why would 
you...?)

In both cases, I have not shown error trapping and exception processing, 
which I would certainly add to the real thing.

In the real code I have wrapped it in a TRY... CATCH block in case there is 
a problem with instantiating it, but it has performed well in test, 
instantiating and running the published COBOL code under .NET InterOP 
Services without any problem at all.

Using objects in C# is REALLY simple and easy and IntelliSense in Visual 
Studio shows all the methods and properties available in the Class, 
automatically.

I see this as a very good example for NOT discarding or rewriting COBOL. It 
works fine... why rewrite it? Just refactor it and continue using it.

> I'll put this on my "wish list" of things to do.  Having such source
> should make the source even MORE portable.

That would be great, Bill. If you manage to get around to doing this, please 
let me have the final source and I'll add it to the download package on 
cobol21. Will make sure you are appropriately acknowledged.

Cheers,

Pete.
```

```
Richard wrote:
> On Dec 5, 12:48 am, "Pete Dashwood"
> <dashw...@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[58 more quoted lines elided]…
> places.

They require switching backwards and forwards to reference and check them. 
Particularly as the IDE won't check spelling or references.

There is no such requirement with the C# code.


>The Repository is equivalent to the 'include' or 'using' lines
> in C# which would normally be counted.

No, it isn't. And I stated there was a reference line excluded from the 
count. The reference line generates the using so it doesn't need to be 
counted twice.
>
> The end-invokes are only required when the invoke is a conditional
> statement, such as having an 'on exception'. As you say you had a TRY
> and CATCH but didn't count those though you did count the 'catch'
> terminator in Cobol.

There was no ON EXCEPTION clause shown in the code (although there would be 
in the real thing). I use scope delimiters as a matter of good programming 
practice (so do many experienced COBOL programmers) whether they are 
"required" or not. My point was that it is much easier to write the 
functionality in C# than it is in COBOL, notwithstanding the number of 
lines. There is nothing disingenuous or misleading in my examples.

>
> The C# would also have to be in a
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
    And the COBOL has to be in a program or Class with a PROGRAM-ID.  I 
believe that must be taken as read and cancels out.
>
>
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>> Class, automatically.

The para above sums up my argument; it isn't really about lines of code 
(although no matter how you write it, COBOL loses on that count, too).

It really comes down to what people are used to and comfortable with. (You 
should be the last person I need to remind of that...)

Once the "familiarity" barrier is broken and people understand the use of 
objects and layers and how they are implemented in modern languages, it is 
really a no brainer. I wrote OO COBOL for over a decade and thought it was 
fine. Now I realize that C# is simply better. (So is Java, and even VB.NET 
is quicker to write than COBOL.)

It isn't necessarily a contest... some people will never want to take the 
steps they would need to in order to break away from COBOL, and some people 
will be happy to continue using COBOL as long as it serves their needs.  I 
don't think they are "wrong". All I'm doing here is showing my own 
experience and encouraging people who might be contemplating a move...

Pete.
```

Bill, 

I do hope your wish list is 'very very short':-).

a) and b) below are what would suit my needs perfectly. I would RDz
compile your source and have a working .exe - right, or download your
.exe? Same for the mainframe? .. so my ideal world ist:

a) in a Windows CMD file ..

set cobfilei=c:\conrad\f023mf.cbl
set cobfileo=c:\conrad\f023mf.lst
cobdata

b) in an OS/JCL stream (if I remember my JCL) ..

//step01 exec pgm=cobdata
//cobfilei dd dsn=prodcopy.sitea(f023mf),disp=shr
//cobfileo dd dsn=ghobbs.proj1.f023mf,disp=(new,catlg,del), etc
//  

When I wrote my own 'cobdata' (mycobpgm, very frustrating and never
adequately working), to check output validity I did this: with
copybook abc.cbl as input I ran mycobpgm. Then I did 'copy abc.cbl'
into a small pgm and the RDz compiler gave me 'length(displacement)'
in its .lst file. Then used a REXX to compare the results - pretty
infallible so long as you believe IBM:-). When the time comes I have
dozens of complex assorted copybooks built especially to test mycobpgm
if you're interested.

A couple of other points I might mention. I would be interested in
seeing additional info on each line produced e.g. is the item
group/elementary, part of an occurs, part of a redefines, both - just
part of my Christmas wish list:-).

Incidentally it only took me 30 minutes or so to IBM'ize the source.
You can have mine if you want.

Graham
---

<wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:

>Pete, Graham, et al,
>  In my spare time, I might try and convert the existing code to an ANSI'85 
…[101 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: My COBDATA problem

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2009-12-05T19:08:28+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7nubmtF3mdl6rU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<g6m3h5deqg28t44ksc1f11eah41eh4cc9u@4ax.com> <7neu4uF3kr7taU1@mid.individual.net> <qr76h5lmlvl9bafb519e3v756o58gaeon4@4ax.com> <7ngs6tF3m16tgU1@mid.individual.net> <dinbh5tb8i5rr9o9cda61ma3a5626t1e0d@4ax.com> <7notr2F3mph8kU1@mid.individual.net> <C%0Sm.317186$Jp1.264537@en-nntp-06.dc1.easynews.com> <8vcjh518i18a2crnqtdigk4kn98doo9ii6@4ax.com>`

```
Graham Hobbs wrote:
> Bill,
>
…[31 more quoted lines elided]…
> part of my Christmas wish list:-).

Those are provided in the current output of COBDATA.
>
> Incidentally it only took me 30 minutes or so to IBM'ize the source.
> You can have mine if you want.

Except that that is the "wrong" code...and it doesn't work :-)

That code is intended purely for illustrative purposes. I already said it 
has bugs in it and it hasn't been maintained. (See the previous stuff 
below...)

The OO version has.  It is the OO version that should be "IBMized". This is 
a simple matter of taking the methods and either making them nested source 
programs, or PERFORMING them as SECTIONS of COBOL code from a standard 
control section or paragraph. At the same time the Data Divisions for the 
methods can be merged into one single Data Division.

Hardly rocket science. The advantage of Bill doing it is that he has 
comprehensive knowledge of what is standard and what isn't, and he doesn't 
"jerk things around" when they don't work, preferring instead to understand 
what caused the problem and fix it... :-)


>
> Graham
…[110 more quoted lines elided]…
>>> "I used to write COBOL...now I can do anything."

Pete.
```

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Looking for *OLD* Microfocus COBOL/2 SCO

- **From:** WaveyDavey <david@regionalservers.com>
- **Date:** 2009-12-01T06:50:54-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<79f0944e-2c8f-44a3-ad65-e97ce109365e@e27g2000yqd.googlegroups.com>`

```
Need a set of media (or images thereof) of MF COBOL/2 dev system for
SCO OpenServer 5.0.5 - media got lost over passing years, and SCO box
is dying. Customer is (was) legit licenced user, but support naturally
long ago expired.
Anyone gold old floppies lying around in a cupboard?
Any ideas anyone?
```

```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Looking for *OLD* Microfocus COBOL/2 SCO

- **From:** WaveyDavey <david@regionalservers.com>
- **Date:** 2009-12-02T09:29:39-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ced46ad-4a03-4ff3-801e-0d05bb19ad1f@g12g2000yqa.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<79f0944e-2c8f-44a3-ad65-e97ce109365e@e27g2000yqd.googlegroups.com> <d2d00005-1c74-4aa2-bf51-9dd1e192e27c@g22g2000prf.googlegroups.com>`

```

> Image the drive and run it as a virtual machine on a new Linux box ?

I'm guessing you've never had to image a SCO box before? It's somewhat
akin to putting your head into a meat grinder.
MS Virtual PC flakes out with shonky (to SCO) disk controller. VMWare
so blasted big I'm still getting into it, but converted ghost image
seems unwilling to boot at all.

What hair I have left, I am slowly pulling out.

David
```

## Thread: "Climategate" code — 48 this month / 77 total

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "Climategate" code

```
On Dec 1, 1:46 pm, "HeyBub" <hey...@NOSPAMgmail.com> wrote:
> Richard wrote:
>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> > produce that forecast.

If we look at what the shonky sceptics did, they abutted the raw sea
level data up to 1920s and the post 1920s raw unadjusted data from up
the hill (and thus cooler) to 'prove' no warming.

In the context of your hyperbole this would be the same as taking the
death valley average temperature up to 1930 and then continuing the
graph with the Denver average temperatures to current day and using
that to 'prove' world temperatures fell.


> You rag on me about the difference between "site" and "station" ["I was
> sited at Ft Polk, but now I'm a station for sore eyes"] now you go on about
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> You really should keep up.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "Climategate" code

- **From:** "HeyBub" <heybub@NOSPAMgmail.com>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<C_qdnSBrUME2M4rWnZ2dnUVZ_hSdnZ2d@earthlink.com>`
- **References:** `<vrqdnQVp7KkjopDWnZ2dnUVZ_q6dnZ2d@earthlink.com> <7n8spaF3looebU1@mid.individual.net> <FPWdnWBubaqVpo3WnZ2dnUVZ_vSdnZ2d@earthlink.com> <ea107402-2315-4233-ae3a-ba696863c017@x5g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <R-6dnc5Pl_rYuIzWnZ2dnUVZ_t6dnZ2d@earthlink.com> <598d7df7-df7f-4818-bec7-84677894d6ef@y10g2000prg.googlegroups.com> <-O6dnYQLH4vxPIzWnZ2dnUVZ_vWdnZ2d@earthlink.com> <5e037f13-106b-4e8a-a905-886189a4f16d@z4g2000prh.googlegroups.com> <nvWdnduk6MCn5I_WnZ2dnUVZ_tadnZ2d@earthlink.com> <f4a185c5-26f6-4b61-8e3f-9279bf172dd5@v15g2000prn.googlegroups.com> <V8qdndeNN7KKaY_WnZ2dnUVZ_hWdnZ2d@earthlink.com> <7ngn1nF3lcgelU1@mid.individual.net> <q_mdnWefQOpDKo7WnZ2dnUVZ_i2dnZ2d@earthlink.com> <8095111f-4294-4873-9351-b8345134c91f@v15g2000prn.googlegroups.com> <dPWdndoLfJft-onWnZ2dnUVZ_q2dnZ2d@earthlink.com> <911a0136-7c4a-46d1-a7d3-2354b057caa8@j11g2000vbi.googlegroups.com>`

```
Richard wrote:
> On Dec 1, 1:46 pm, "HeyBub" <hey...@NOSPAMgmail.com> wrote:
>> Richard wrote:
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> that to 'prove' world temperatures fell.

Now you bring up what the skeptics did. Until now, no one has said anything 
about the activities of the anti-warming crowd. We were discussing the 
criminal fraud perpetrated by the CRU and their co-conspirators in NZ.

I'm not interested in what the skeptics say or did. Your random 
dart-throwing in an attempt to find a situation in which what you allege is 
meaningful is simply appalling.

My contention, illustrated by exaggeration, was that it is simply not good 
science to take data from one physical location, add some sort of constant 
to it, and expect it to accurately portray reality from a distinctly 
different location. Hell, it's not "science" at all; it's deception, 
obfuscation, and, since they were taking money from the government, criminal 
fraud.

The people involved shoud be cremated (even if it contributes to 'global 
warming'), and their ashes scattered. Take no chances.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "Climategate" code

_(thread depth: 19)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()
- **Date:** 2009-12-03T13:46:18+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<hf8ffa$d0u$1@reader1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<vrqdnQVp7KkjopDWnZ2dnUVZ_q6dnZ2d@earthlink.com> <dPWdndoLfJft-onWnZ2dnUVZ_q2dnZ2d@earthlink.com> <911a0136-7c4a-46d1-a7d3-2354b057caa8@j11g2000vbi.googlegroups.com> <C_qdnSBrUME2M4rWnZ2dnUVZ_hSdnZ2d@earthlink.com>`

```
In article <C_qdnSBrUME2M4rWnZ2dnUVZ_hSdnZ2d@earthlink.com>,
HeyBub <heybub@NOSPAMgmail.com> wrote:

[snip]

>My contention, illustrated by exaggeration, was that it is simply not good 
>science to take data from one physical location, add some sort of constant 
>to it, and expect it to accurately portray reality from a distinctly 
>different location.

Take:

a sit-down by the seaside
one pot of water
heat to a full boil
add one egg
wait three minutes
enjoy an egg of a cosistency called 'a three-minute egg'.

Change the location from sea level to 10,000 feet above sea level.  To get 
the same consistency one needs five minutes cooking.

>Hell, it's not "science" at all; it's deception, 
>obfuscation, and, since they were taking money from the government, criminal 
>fraud.

Anyone with sufficient formal training to be familiar with the qualifier 
of 'fifty-five Paris feet' might recognise that changing *only the place* 
where observations are made can change the results *and* be accounted for 
by arithmetic constants... that's been 'science' for at least a 
century-and-a-half.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "Climategate" code

_(thread depth: 20)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2009-12-03T08:56:57-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8onfh5db8fjbkj0qui9igqn6v7fq3e15fc@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<vrqdnQVp7KkjopDWnZ2dnUVZ_q6dnZ2d@earthlink.com> <dPWdndoLfJft-onWnZ2dnUVZ_q2dnZ2d@earthlink.com> <911a0136-7c4a-46d1-a7d3-2354b057caa8@j11g2000vbi.googlegroups.com> <C_qdnSBrUME2M4rWnZ2dnUVZ_hSdnZ2d@earthlink.com> <hf8ffa$d0u$1@reader1.panix.com>`

```
On Thu, 3 Dec 2009 13:46:18 +0000 (UTC), docdwarf@panix.com () wrote:

>a sit-down by the seaside
>one pot of water
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>the same consistency one needs five minutes cooking.

I lived a couple of years in Mexico City during the Kennedy
administration.    At that time, US made cake mixes were available
with Spanish language instructions stuck on the box - modified for
Mexico City altitude.    My parents complained that the coffee wasn't
hot enough for their taste.  I am at work in Boulder Colorado at the
moment, but haven't seen recipe modifiers - I expect the mountains are
different.    I do know I have to have a bigger boil to cook pasta
here - the boiling water isn't quite hot enough.
```

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()

"""On the other hand, somebody graphed the raw temperature readings

> We were discussing the

…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> different location.

The locations in Wellington are just a minute or two apart. Any wind
could well pass the same air by both, but as the air has had to travel
'up the hill' or 'down the hill' the it will obey Boyle's Law and will
change temperature without losing or gaining heat.

If you cannot understand such basic science then you should remove
yourself until you do.


> Hell, it's not "science" at all; it's deception,
> obfuscation, and, since they were taking money from the government, criminal
> fraud.

described as "deception and obfuscation" and that nothing you have
said could be described as 'science' then you are entire disengenuous,
amongst other failings.


> The people involved shoud be cremated (even if it contributes to 'global
> warming'), and their ashes scattered. Take no chances.

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<47b8d7fb-d3bd-4e21-ad3e-71706bdb42ff@x25g2000prf.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<nvWdnduk6MCn5I_WnZ2dnUVZ_tadnZ2d@earthlink.com> <f4a185c5-26f6-4b61-8e3f-9279bf172dd5@v15g2000prn.googlegroups.com> <V8qdndeNN7KKaY_WnZ2dnUVZ_hWdnZ2d@earthlink.com> <7ngn1nF3lcgelU1@mid.individual.net> <q_mdnWefQOpDKo7WnZ2dnUVZ_i2dnZ2d@earthlink.com> <8095111f-4294-4873-9351-b8345134c91f@v15g2000prn.googlegroups.com> <dPWdndoLfJft-onWnZ2dnUVZ_q2dnZ2d@earthlink.com> <911a0136-7c4a-46d1-a7d3-2354b057caa8@j11g2000vbi.googlegroups.com> <gvdoh5t2aevkefmgbjlbpmssh1ovmc9cds@4ax.com>`

```
On Dec 7, 12:04 pm, Clark F Morris <cfmpub...@ns.sympatico.ca> wrote:
> On Wed, 2 Dec 2009 10:04:37 -0800 (PST), Richard <rip...@Azonic.co.nz>
> wrote:

>
> >On Dec 1, 1:46 pm, "HeyBub" <hey...@NOSPAMgmail.com> wrote:
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> adjustment factor used?

The temperature difference between the two sites is the result of the
adiabatic lapse. In other words what is specified by Boyles Law when
there are changes in pressure due to change in altitude.

As the altitude difference between these two sites is fixed and
relatively small then the difference in altitude pressure will not
vary to any significant degree, both sites will have the same base
atmospheric pressure (measured at sea level).

You can do the calculations to determine the theoretical variation in
temperature differences.

As these were used as averages then any tiny theoretical variation in
the differences (as distinct from the variations in temperature)
caused by cyclone and anti-cyclone pressure changes would be evened
out.

What is the real issue is that the skeptics pasted together the raw
unadjusted temperatures and made fraudulent and uninformed claims.
```

Pete Dashwood wrote:
>
> A clear and simple explanation.
>
> Thanks Richard.
>>

If adiabatic pressure were the only variable between the two locations, 
there might be some accuracy in the adjustment. But that's not the only 
difference. It's not like they put the temperature guage on the top of a 
twenty story building and adjusted for 200 feet - they changed the location 
of the instrument cluster. Much more than elevation could be involved: 
Relative humidity, average wind speed, surrounding area heat sinks or 
radiation proclivity, reflected sunlight, blah-blah-blah.

They moved the station.

- **References:** `<nvWdnduk6MCn5I_WnZ2dnUVZ_tadnZ2d@earthlink.com> <f4a185c5-26f6-4b61-8e3f-9279bf172dd5@v15g2000prn.googlegroups.com> <V8qdndeNN7KKaY_WnZ2dnUVZ_hWdnZ2d@earthlink.com> <7ngn1nF3lcgelU1@mid.individual.net> <q_mdnWefQOpDKo7WnZ2dnUVZ_i2dnZ2d@earthlink.com> <8095111f-4294-4873-9351-b8345134c91f@v15g2000prn.googlegroups.com> <dPWdndoLfJft-onWnZ2dnUVZ_q2dnZ2d@earthlink.com> <911a0136-7c4a-46d1-a7d3-2354b057caa8@j11g2000vbi.googlegroups.com> <gvdoh5t2aevkefmgbjlbpmssh1ovmc9cds@4ax.com> <47b8d7fb-d3bd-4e21-ad3e-71706bdb42ff@x25g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <7o2vngF3og2vlU1@mid.individual.net> <b82dnTGx3sUBKoPWnZ2dnUVZ_gydnZ2d@earthlink.com>`

```
On Dec 9, 9:31 am, "HeyBub" <hey...@NOSPAMgmail.com> wrote:
> Pete Dashwood wrote:
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> of the instrument cluster.

They moved the station up the hill.

The clueless skeptics attempted to use the raw figures
_with_no_adjustment_at_all_ which is clearly wrong. Even you finally
understand that adjustment is needed for altitude.

> Much more than elevation could be involved:
> Relative humidity, average wind speed, surrounding area heat sinks or
> radiation proclivity, reflected sunlight, blah-blah-blah.
>

As all those will change from time to time, day to day and season to
season for _ONE_ location then what point are you trying to make ?
Stations are deliberately positioned to reduce all those effects, in
fact it was probably because the city was growing in the 1920s with
reclamation and new buildings that decided them to move it to a place
where the effects would not increase.

The data was put together as _averages_. On average all those will
balance out.

The airport station, which is just 5 km away and at about sea level,
was compared to the readings for several decades from the 1950s on and
found to have a consistent difference attributable to the altitude.

These locations are all in the _same_ weather patterns.

Just because you can think of something does not mean that it has not
already been thought of and dealt with decades ago.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "Climategate" code

_(thread depth: 23)_

- **From:** "HeyBub" <heybub@NOSPAMgmail.com>
- **Date:** 2009-12-09T08:30:10-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<NK6dnRaIH6vrKYLWnZ2dnUVZ_iydnZ2d@earthlink.com>`
- **References:** `<nvWdnduk6MCn5I_WnZ2dnUVZ_tadnZ2d@earthlink.com> <f4a185c5-26f6-4b61-8e3f-9279bf172dd5@v15g2000prn.googlegroups.com> <V8qdndeNN7KKaY_WnZ2dnUVZ_hWdnZ2d@earthlink.com> <7ngn1nF3lcgelU1@mid.individual.net> <q_mdnWefQOpDKo7WnZ2dnUVZ_i2dnZ2d@earthlink.com> <8095111f-4294-4873-9351-b8345134c91f@v15g2000prn.googlegroups.com> <dPWdndoLfJft-onWnZ2dnUVZ_q2dnZ2d@earthlink.com> <911a0136-7c4a-46d1-a7d3-2354b057caa8@j11g2000vbi.googlegroups.com> <gvdoh5t2aevkefmgbjlbpmssh1ovmc9cds@4ax.com> <47b8d7fb-d3bd-4e21-ad3e-71706bdb42ff@x25g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <7o2vngF3og2vlU1@mid.individual.net> <b82dnTGx3sUBKoPWnZ2dnUVZ_gydnZ2d@earthlink.com> <4be1258d-34d7-46d5-aed9-39ae0b924b64@u25g2000prh.googlegroups.com>`

```
Richard wrote:
>
> They moved the station up the hill.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Skeptics don't disagree with the concept of adjustments (what the 
climatologists call "inhomogeneity"). Legitimate adjustments can be made for 
such things as: instrumentation, environment changes where the station is 
located, etc. What cannot be countenanced is outright fraud - fudging the 
numbers to achieve a pre-determined result.

I refer you to an analysis of Australian temperature data involving the 
"raw" data which was, erroneously NOT deleted, compared to the "homogenized" 
or adjusted temperatures.

http://wattsupwiththat.com/2009/12/08/the-smoking-gun-at-darwin-zero/

Money quotes:

"YIKES! Before getting homogenized [adjusted], temperatures in Darwin were 
falling at 0.7 Celcius per century . but after the homogenization, they were 
warming at 1.2 Celcius per century. And the adjustment that they made was 
over two degrees per century . when those guys "adjust", they don't mess 
around. And the adjustment is an odd shape, with the adjustment first going 
stepwise, then climbing roughly to stop at 2.4C."

and

"Yikes again, double yikes! What on earth justifies that adjustment? How can 
they do that? We have five different records covering Darwin from 1941 on. 
They all agree almost exactly. Why adjust them at all? They've just added a 
huge artificial totally imaginary trend to the last half of the raw data! 
Now it looks like the IPCC diagram in Figure 1, all right . but a six degree 
per century trend? And in the shape of a regular stepped pyramid climbing to 
heaven? What's up with that?"

Then the conclusion:

"And with the Latin saying "Falsus in unum, falsus in omis" (false in one, 
false in all) as our guide, until all of the station "adjustments" are 
examined, adjustments of CRU, GHCN, and GISS alike, we can't trust anyone 
using homogenized numbers."
```

On Sun, 6 Dec 2009 15:47:30 -0800 (PST), Richard <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
wrote:

>On Dec 7, 12:04ï¿½pm, Clark F Morris <cfmpub...@ns.sympatico.ca> wrote:
>> On Wed, 2 Dec 2009 10:04:37 -0800 (PST), Richard <rip...@Azonic.co.nz>
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
>atmospheric pressure (measured at sea level).

That assumes that altitude differences are the only differences.  I
live 5 miles (8 kilometers) from the Bay of Fundy on the other side of
a range of 500 foot high hills at a higher elevation and have higher
temperatures most of the year than those at a lower altitude near the
coast.  I would want to know a lot more about the two locations (and
why the measuring point was moved) before I have confidence that an
altitude correction is the only one that is needed. 
>
>You can do the calculations to determine the theoretical variation in
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>unadjusted temperatures and made fraudulent and uninformed claims.
```

- **Date:** 2009-12-07T13:12:36+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7o2vjkF3o3ahjU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<nvWdnduk6MCn5I_WnZ2dnUVZ_tadnZ2d@earthlink.com> <f4a185c5-26f6-4b61-8e3f-9279bf172dd5@v15g2000prn.googlegroups.com> <V8qdndeNN7KKaY_WnZ2dnUVZ_hWdnZ2d@earthlink.com> <7ngn1nF3lcgelU1@mid.individual.net> <q_mdnWefQOpDKo7WnZ2dnUVZ_i2dnZ2d@earthlink.com> <8095111f-4294-4873-9351-b8345134c91f@v15g2000prn.googlegroups.com> <dPWdndoLfJft-onWnZ2dnUVZ_q2dnZ2d@earthlink.com> <911a0136-7c4a-46d1-a7d3-2354b057caa8@j11g2000vbi.googlegroups.com> <gvdoh5t2aevkefmgbjlbpmssh1ovmc9cds@4ax.com>`

```
Clark F Morris wrote:
> On Wed, 2 Dec 2009 10:04:37 -0800 (PST), Richard <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
> wrote:
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
> adjustment factor used?

Those are all very good questions, Clark.

I think the .8 of a degree is a "mean" variation that would be based mainly 
on adiabatic compensation, and local weather conditions, but I don't KNOW 
that.

My own feeling is that it is a "fair" adjustment and I don't think it has 
been done as part of a conspiracy to fudge weather data in support of Global 
Warming.

Again, I don't KNOW that, either.

I guess it depends on how passionately you feel about GW (I'm on the fence) 
and how important the weather is in your life. :-)

For me, for now, I'm not observing anything untoward in the weather here. 
Today is 24 and pleasant.

Id rather be enjoying it than worrying about it... :-)

(Yes, I know that it is better to prevent problems than to deal with them 
once they've arrived, but it seems to me enough people are doing the 
worrying without actually needing my help...)

<unreferenced remainder snipped>

Pete.
```

Alistair wrote:

…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> alt.fundamentalistsbelieveingineverythingGodsays?

I can't imagine at what data you were looking at 30 years ago, unless it 
came from the IPCC. From 1940 until 1974 the average Global temperature 
dropped by 2.7�F.

The data prior to 1974 showed a dramatic COOLING. If fact, the coming ice 
age was a Time Magazine cover story. Read the article from June of that 
year.

http://www.junkscience.com/mar06/Time_AnotherIceAge_June241974.pdf

This entire thread started with a comment about the unintelligible code used 
by the Climate Research Unit in East Anglia. One experienced programmer 
spent a YEAR trying to make sense of the programs and his journal was part 
of the leaked documents.

On Dec 3, 9:20 pm, "HeyBub" <hey...@NOSPAMgmail.com> wrote:
> Alistair wrote:
>
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
> of the leaked documents.

The data shown to me was regarding the temperatures in the
Mediterranean region. The data showede a clear thirty year cycle of
increasing temeprature followed by a brief return to a level close to
BUT NOT EQUIVALENT TO OR LOWER THAN the starting temperature from the
previous cycle. So there was a local 30 year cycle (not global
warming) and a fail to return to the starting point (global warming).
The data had been extracted by hand by an undergraduate student and
graphed, again by hand, by the same student.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: IS NUMERIC check for SPACES

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2009-12-01T08:25:56-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9bdah59bd3mm0f2l55hfssk4bnt3n7ifs6@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<ac1c02d1-64a3-400b-9c9a-63989d519a2b@f16g2000yqm.googlegroups.com> <bR0Rm.277892$sz1.97428@en-nntp-10.dc1.easynews.com>`

```
On Mon, 30 Nov 2009 22:21:24 -0600, "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:

>Easy answer, when running in "standard conformance" mode, IF NUMERIC will 
>fail when a field contains spaces.  It will also have "unpredictable 
>results" if a field deinfed as numeric includes spaces when compared to 
>SPACERS or anything else - at run-time.

I never cared for the definition of IF NUMERIC.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: IS NUMERIC check for SPACES

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2009-12-01T09:40:28-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<NNaRm.292260$uO.92153@en-nntp-09.dc1.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<ac1c02d1-64a3-400b-9c9a-63989d519a2b@f16g2000yqm.googlegroups.com> <bR0Rm.277892$sz1.97428@en-nntp-10.dc1.easynews.com> <9bdah59bd3mm0f2l55hfssk4bnt3n7ifs6@4ax.com>`

```
One addendum to my original response.  I can't remember (and haven't looked 
it up today) whether the '02 Standard allows an "If numeric" test on a 
numeric-edited field,  If so, then where the picutre string has "Z" or if 
the data definition has "blank when zero", then IF numeric would "pass" when 
spaces were in such a field.  As I say, I don't remember is this is either 
Standard conforming or allowed by any, many, or no compilers - buti it does 
make sense for spaces to be "allowed' where the data definition says they 
are allowed.

(I am out of town and can't easiy est or research this.  If I get a "better" 
answer later in the week, I will post that)

#### ↳ Re: IS NUMERIC check for SPACES

- **From:** spambait@milmac.com (Doug Miller)
- **Date:** 2009-12-02T04:24:34+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<hf4q6j$n1m$4@news.eternal-september.org>`
- **References:** `<ac1c02d1-64a3-400b-9c9a-63989d519a2b@f16g2000yqm.googlegroups.com>`

```
In article <ac1c02d1-64a3-400b-9c9a-63989d519a2b@f16g2000yqm.googlegroups.com>, yogi <yogibad@gmail.com> wrote:
>Hi All,
>            I had a question regarding blanks or SPACES in numeric
>fields. 

In the time it took you to post this question and read the responses, you 
could have written, compiled, and tested a program that would show you the 
answer -- and you'd have learned a lot more from doing so than you will by 
reading the responses here. Having said that... here are the answers to your 
questions:

>Would SPACES pass the IS NUMERIC test used for checking if the
>value is numeric or not. 

No. Performing the IS NUMERIC test on a field that contains spaces will 
generate an exception.

>Since SPACES can be compared arithmetically

No, it can't.

>(> SPACES), 

That's a lexical comparison, *not* an arithmetic comparison. That simply tests 
to see whether a particular byte falls before, or after, space in the 
collating sequence.

>I was wondering if it would pass the IS NUMERIC test.

Of course not. SPACE is not a numeric digit. Numeric digits are 0 through 9.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: IS NUMERIC check for SPACES

- **From:** Richard <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2009-12-01T23:01:40-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<603d52d3-a595-4e19-a954-154aba429fdd@c34g2000yqn.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<ac1c02d1-64a3-400b-9c9a-63989d519a2b@f16g2000yqm.googlegroups.com> <hf4q6j$n1m$4@news.eternal-september.org>`

```
On Dec 2, 5:24 pm, spamb...@milmac.com (Doug Miller) wrote:
> In article <ac1c02d1-64a3-400b-9c9a-63989d519...@f16g2000yqm.googlegroups.com>, yogi <yogi...@gmail.com> wrote:
>
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> generate an exception.

The result of the test will be 'false'. 'Generating an exception'
would rather defeat to purpose of doing the test (which can be to
avoid an exception).

>
> >Since SPACES can be compared arithmetically
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> Of course not. SPACE is not a numeric digit. Numeric digits are 0 through 9.

Note, however, that the sign representation may lead to other
characters in the field, usually an 'overpunched' digit on the leading
or trailing character or a + or - if 'sign separate'.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: IS NUMERIC check for SPACES

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()
- **Date:** 2009-12-02T14:21:49+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<hf5t5t$og3$1@reader1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<ac1c02d1-64a3-400b-9c9a-63989d519a2b@f16g2000yqm.googlegroups.com> <hf4q6j$n1m$4@news.eternal-september.org>`

```
In article <hf4q6j$n1m$4@news.eternal-september.org>,
Doug Miller <spambait@milmac.com> wrote:

[snip]

>Of course not. SPACE is not a numeric digit. Numeric digits are 0 through 9.

Ow... Mr Miller, it might be best to stay with the question of 'will 
(thing) pass an IS NUMERIC test?' and avoid the depths of what constitutes 
'numeric digits'.  The characters 0 through 9 are, as we all know, 
represted in DISPLAY format by the hexadecimal characters X'F0' through 
X'F9' (unless one is dealing with ASCII, in which case they are X'C0' 
through X'C9') or the binary equivalents 1111 0000 through 1111 1111 (or 
ASCII 1100 0000 through 1100 1001).

Now my memory is, admittedly, porous but I recall that IBM mainframe 
compilers (IKFCBL00 and IGYCRCTL) will successfully test COMP-3 (packed 
decimal format) fields with IS NUMERIC... and as a COMP-3 field, in my 
experience, often has its least significant nibble reserved for the 
field's sign (usually C for positive, D for negative) then a COMP-3 field 
containing X'123D' may pass an IS NUMERIC test while not, by your 
standard, containing an numeric digit.

Next week... Are Other Representational Systems Numeric or Not?  After 
All, This *is* the XXIth Century!

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: IS NUMERIC check for SPACES

- **Date:** 2009-12-03T19:51:27+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<hf94sb$aql$2@news.eternal-september.org>`
- **References:** `<ac1c02d1-64a3-400b-9c9a-63989d519a2b@f16g2000yqm.googlegroups.com> <hf4q6j$n1m$4@news.eternal-september.org> <hf5t5t$og3$1@reader1.panix.com>`

>compilers (IKFCBL00 and IGYCRCTL) will successfully test COMP-3 (packed 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…

1, 2, and 3 are numeric digits, no? The sign nybble is ignored.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: IS NUMERIC check for SPACES

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()
- **Date:** 2009-12-03T20:16:43+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<hf96bb$q98$9@reader1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<ac1c02d1-64a3-400b-9c9a-63989d519a2b@f16g2000yqm.googlegroups.com> <hf4q6j$n1m$4@news.eternal-september.org> <hf5t5t$og3$1@reader1.panix.com> <hf94sb$aql$2@news.eternal-september.org>`

```
In article <hf94sb$aql$2@news.eternal-september.org>,
Doug Miller <spambait@milmac.com> wrote:
>In article <hf5t5t$og3$1@reader1.panix.com>, docdwarf@panix.com () wrote:
>>Now my memory is, admittedly, porous but I recall that IBM mainframe 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>1, 2, and 3 are numeric digits, no? The sign nybble is ignored.

If the sign is ignored then a COMP-3 field containing X'0000' would pass 
an IS NUMERIC test... and as I recall - porous memory and all - it 
doesn't.  COMP (binary) fields, no problem... but COMP-3 needs a valid 
sign.

Perhaps some testing is in order.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: IS NUMERIC check for SPACES

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** slade <jnjsle1@optonline.net>
- **Date:** 2009-12-11T19:47:58-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2a82e1bc-8e40-4704-8206-c76ee94a5f0a@n13g2000vbe.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<ac1c02d1-64a3-400b-9c9a-63989d519a2b@f16g2000yqm.googlegroups.com> <hf4q6j$n1m$4@news.eternal-september.org> <hf5t5t$og3$1@reader1.panix.com> <hf94sb$aql$2@news.eternal-september.org> <hf96bb$q98$9@reader1.panix.com>`

```
On Dec 3, 3:16 pm, docdw...@panix.com () wrote:
> In article <hf94sb$aq...@news.eternal-september.org>,
>
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> DD

In an IBM mainframe environment, depending on the NUMPROC/NUMCLS COBOL
compiler options selected, your sign choices are limited, at best, to
X'A" thru X'F'; at worst, to X'F'.  Any other nybble will result in a
"FALSE" return from IF NUMERIC.

So, as Doc said, the sign for numeric DISPLAY (and COMP-3) fields is
not ignored; X'F1F203' will generate a "FALSE" return.

If the item is defined w/SIGN IS SEPARATE (not an issue here,
apparently) there are other criteria.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: IS NUMERIC check for SPACES

_(thread depth: 6)_

code and results, so that experiment's reproducibility be verified?

DD

<a id="t-ffe5986c39"></a>
## Thread: Accelerated legacy code — 21 msgs

_Topics: [`COBOL's future, legacy, and obsolescence`](topics.md#future)_

### Accelerated legacy code

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2009-12-03T09:27:26-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<gapfh5dfauho0cqi9r45dkev3nvcr2htt1@4ax.com>`

```
We tend to associate "legacy" with "mainframe".    But it ain't
necessarily so.

From my limited exposure, the large new systems I have seen have lots
of code which doesn't fit new standards, but is kept around because
redesign and replacement is much more difficult when anything can
touch anything.

I'm calling it "accelerated legacy code".  
```

#### ↳ Re: Accelerated legacy code

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2009-12-04T13:11:16+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7nr2d6F3jkdqhU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<gapfh5dfauho0cqi9r45dkev3nvcr2htt1@4ax.com>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
> We tend to associate "legacy" with "mainframe".    But it ain't
> necessarily so.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> I'm calling it "accelerated legacy code".

I used to think "legacy" only applied to mainframe until I was given a task 
to "keep legacy running while we develop and implement a new solution" for a 
major Utility in the U.K.

They were replacing their existing systems (which did include an IBM 
mainframe, but were mainly client/server) with the Siebel package. 
(30,000,000 UK pounds). I had a team of around 10 people and found to my 
astonishment that most of the applications were written as stored procedures 
on a number of  distributed SQL Server DBs. All of this code was considered 
"legacy" and this was in the late 1990s... The Business were generally out 
of patience with IT, the team were dispirited and demotivated because they 
had not even been offered the chance to work on the New Technology, and most 
of them considered their jobs were gone.

We not only kept it running, we improved it out of sight and the Business 
became very fond of us. (I spent a deal of time lunching with Business 
managers and listening to them.)  I managed to get most of the team (the 
ones who wanted to; two were pretty disillusioned with the company and left 
as soon as they could get another job) redeployed (after training) onto 
Siebel support and, as the existing systems stabilised, they were able to 
spend time with the Siebel implementation. By the time my contract finished 
this "discarded" group had made some major contributions to the Siebel 
development, contributing their business experience and gaining Siebel tech 
skills.  I don't know how successful the overall Siebel implementation was, 
but the "legacy" code was in good shape having fallen from over 900 known 
problems to around 40, and despite a very limited budget (they were 
understandably loath to spend money on existing systems), we had managed to 
meet several urgent business requirements for new functionality.

I never saw "legacy" in quite the same light again, after that exercise.

Pete.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Accelerated legacy code

- **From:** Cydrome Leader <presence@MUNGEpanix.com>
- **Date:** 2009-12-05T22:45:34+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<hfenqd$p2u$1@reader1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<gapfh5dfauho0cqi9r45dkev3nvcr2htt1@4ax.com> <7nr2d6F3jkdqhU1@mid.individual.net>`

```
Pete Dashwood <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
> Howard Brazee wrote:
>> We tend to associate "legacy" with "mainframe".    But it ain't
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> major Utility in the U.K.

heck, there can be legacy code and applications from even a few years ago.

One example is a CRM/ticket system that only works with IE6. Users can't 
even access it as the desktop build is beyond IE6. It simply doesn't work, 
even with IE7 or IE8 in legacy mode. It doesn't work with other browsers 
at all.

It's looking like some windows 98 machines will have to be kept on hand to 
use the thing, and it the data on it has to be accessible for X more 
years, even if nobody knows how to run it and it's on hardware with no 
more support. It has to be less than 5 years old too.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Accelerated legacy code

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2009-12-07T13:01:30+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7o2uuqF3o08saU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<gapfh5dfauho0cqi9r45dkev3nvcr2htt1@4ax.com> <7nr2d6F3jkdqhU1@mid.individual.net> <hfenqd$p2u$1@reader1.panix.com>`

```
Cydrome Leader wrote:
> Pete Dashwood <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>> Howard Brazee wrote:
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>

That's a pretty bad situation and a lesson to us all. I think most 
responsible web developers check for at least SOME fundamental cross Browser 
capability, or issue a disclaimer on their sites. With modern tools it isn't 
TOO hard to check things across Browsers (although it is definitely extra 
work)... 5 years ago such tools did exist (MacroMedia have had this 
capability in Dreamweaver for more than 5 years, just as one example).

> It's looking like some windows 98 machines will have to be kept on
> hand to use the thing, and it the data on it has to be accessible for
> X more years, even if nobody knows how to run it and it's on hardware
> with no more support. It has to be less than 5 years old too.

I have to wonder why anyone would take delivery of such a system. I guess 
they saw functionality they needed and decided the tech stuff didn't 
matter...

I would have thought it would be pretty easy to replace a CRM system; there 
are dozens of very good packages around that provide that functionality.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Accelerated legacy code

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Paul <paul-nospamatall.raulerson@mac.com>
- **Date:** 2009-12-08T22:15:04-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4b1f2447$0$5323$bbae4d71@news.suddenlink.net>`
- **References:** `<gapfh5dfauho0cqi9r45dkev3nvcr2htt1@4ax.com> <7nr2d6F3jkdqhU1@mid.individual.net> <hfenqd$p2u$1@reader1.panix.com> <7o2uuqF3o08saU1@mid.individual.net>`

```
On 2009-12-06 18:01:30 -0600, "Pete Dashwood" 
<dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> said:

> Cydrome Leader wrote:
>> Pete Dashwood <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[43 more quoted lines elided]…
> Pete.

In general, I would find myself questioning the facts in this case.  
Five years ago, in 2004, XP
would have been the standard build. Windows 98 was at that point, 
around six years old.

And even if the facts supported the idea of the necessity of keeping 
Win98 machines around,
in 2009, those "machines" would be virtual machines running on top of 
Windows Server 2003,
Windows Server 2008, Linux, or MacOS.

-Paul
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Accelerated legacy code

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Cydrome Leader <presence@MUNGEpanix.com>
- **Date:** 2009-12-09T21:59:20+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<hfp6jo$10l$2@reader1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<gapfh5dfauho0cqi9r45dkev3nvcr2htt1@4ax.com> <7nr2d6F3jkdqhU1@mid.individual.net> <hfenqd$p2u$1@reader1.panix.com> <7o2uuqF3o08saU1@mid.individual.net> <4b1f2447$0$5323$bbae4d71@news.suddenlink.net>`

```
Paul <paul-nospamatall.raulerson@mac.com> wrote:
> On 2009-12-06 18:01:30 -0600, "Pete Dashwood" 
> <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> said:
…[51 more quoted lines elided]…
> around six years old.

I'd love to share more facts, but cannot do it on usenet. This is only one 
of several nonsense applications I've had to work with. 

There's even current sofware out there that only works with IE6. There 
really is SAN management software from Hitachi that cannot be used to add 
storage to a host as it it can't detect that you checked checkboxes in a 
web based form, in anything other than IE6.

> And even if the facts supported the idea of the necessity of keeping 
> Win98 machines around,
> in 2009, those "machines" would be virtual machines running on top of 
> Windows Server 2003,
> Windows Server 2008, Linux, or MacOS.

Any organization clever enough to have such junk in the first place isn't 
going to spend the money on virtualized browsers on some 2003 mega server.

Getting IE6 will involve finding half broken machines in some forgotten 
pile of office equipement that nobody remembered to throw away.

There's sometimes a huge gap in the right way to do something and how it 
actually gets done inside an organization.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Accelerated legacy code

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Cydrome Leader <presence@MUNGEpanix.com>
- **Date:** 2009-12-21T01:58:27+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<hgmko3$mdi$3@reader1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<gapfh5dfauho0cqi9r45dkev3nvcr2htt1@4ax.com> <7nr2d6F3jkdqhU1@mid.individual.net> <hfenqd$p2u$1@reader1.panix.com> <7o2uuqF3o08saU1@mid.individual.net> <4b1f2447$0$5323$bbae4d71@news.suddenlink.net> <hfp6jo$10l$2@reader1.panix.com> <4b208ecb$0$5364$bbae4d71@news.suddenlink.net>`

```
Paul <paul-nospamatall.raulerson@mac.com> wrote:
> On 2009-12-09 15:59:20 -0600, Cydrome Leader <presence@MUNGEpanix.com> said:
> 
…[89 more quoted lines elided]…
> and the least expensive way.   The two desires are usually not in opposition.

The least expensive way is not a 2003 mega server as I said before. Having 
to pull old desktops from a trash pile will happen before "just load 2003 
server" happens.

Also, the right way isn't always the least expensive way. Take laptops in 
a business environment.

you can get 17 people the cheapest on sale refurb machines over the course 
of a year as a rolling upgrade, or just get everybody the same thinkpad, 
and actually be able to image the machines as needed, or replace any unit 
or part on site if you want to, plus know parts will be available forever. 

It's also nice to have machines that can withstand some beating, even if 
they cost $1000 more. It's still cheaper than manning extra people at a 
help desk and having users paralyzed when their machine does fail.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Accelerated legacy code

_(thread depth: 4)_

in VMs for the people needing access to that application (VMWare
explicitly supports Win2K)?  You could even do Win98 in a VM, but why?

Sorry for jumping in so late - and, of course, others might not be - but 
it sounds like the New Pair o' Dimes repeats the Old Two Bits in that 'The 
system is not to be considered for decommissioning until two weeks after 
the last user dies.'

>
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>explicitly supports Win2K)?  You could even do Win98 in a VM, but why?

Mr Wessel, it sounds like the client ('client' in the human sense, the 
organisation/folks who had the system built and need to use it) suffers 
from some of the Standard Conditions:  'If it ain't broke, don't fix it... 
and if it can be work *at all* (reach left hand over head to scratch right 
ear) then it ain't broke' and 'Change stuff?  Our People are comfortable 
with the way things are now and Change Costs *Money*!' are two which come 
readily to mind.

DD
```

docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

> robertwessel2@yahoo.com <robertwessel2@yahoo.com> wrote:

Even when it was new, somebody should have stepped in and said, "wait, 
this design is stupid". Obviously that never happened.

>>While that's an unfortunate situation, Win98 on ancient hardware seems
>>like a solution engineered to be as bad as possible.  Why not do Win2K
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> readily to mind.

That's pretty much it. As things "work" there's no need to "fix" it. It's 
not even clear if it could be fixed, and what the benefit would even be in 
the first place if it was.

So until people forget it exists, or it just self destructs, it has to be 
left alone.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Accelerated legacy code

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()
- **Date:** 2009-12-10T11:46:30+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<hfqn2m$91q$1@reader1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<gapfh5dfauho0cqi9r45dkev3nvcr2htt1@4ax.com> <a42c1d80-1c6f-4504-8a32-86468e8fdcbb@v25g2000yqk.googlegroups.com> <hflo3a$a3c$1@reader1.panix.com> <hfp6s2$10l$3@reader1.panix.com>`

```
In article <hfp6s2$10l$3@reader1.panix.com>,
Cydrome Leader  <presence@MUNGEpanix.com> wrote:
>docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
>> In article
><a42c1d80-1c6f-4504-8a32-86468e8fdcbb@v25g2000yqk.googlegroups.com>,
>> robertwessel2@yahoo.com <robertwessel2@yahoo.com> wrote:
>>>On Dec 5, 4:45?pm, Cydrome Leader <prese...@MUNGEpanix.com> wrote:

[snip]

>>>> It's looking like some windows 98 machines will have to be kept on hand to
>>>> use the thing, and it the data on it has to be accessible for X more
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>this design is stupid". Obviously that never happened.

There might be a Very Good Reason for this, Mr Wessel; as Samuel Goldwyn 
is reputed to have said, 'I don't want to be surrounded by yes-men, I want 
people who'll give me their honest opinions even if it costs them their 
jobs'... or words to that effect.

>
>>>While that's an unfortunate situation, Win98 on ancient hardware seems
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>the first place if it was.

The benefit would be getting data off machines which have reached the end 
of their useful lives (see above, '...the data on it has to be accessible 
for X more years...', which may be a Legal Requirement - Payroll comes to 
mind, pay data of varying kinds must be kept for anywhere from two to 
fifty-six years in the United States of America) and on to machines which 
still have a bit of use left in their lives.

When I'm not putting out fires I have a tendency to ask questions like... 
'What happens if someone isn't here to put out a fire and you come in 
tomorrow to a charred hulk of a building?  Has anyone cobbled together a 
COB (Continuation of Business) plan?'

(Responses I've gotten include 'No', 'Yes, I keep a fresh copy of my 
resume at home' and 'What, are you threatening to set fire to the place?')

>
>So until people forget it exists, or it just self destructs, it has to be 
>left alone.

... and leaving it alone will guarantee that it will be forgotten 
(development is so much more interesting than maintenance, anyway) and 
that it will 'self destruct', in that the chips inside the machines will 
eventually fail.  The machines aren't what is important, the data are; 
even though Hollerith cards were first used in the 1890 US census the 
first 'computerised' census was not attempted until 1970 and when they 
1980 census was being considered folks found out there existed two 
machines in the world that could deal with the 1970 data... one was in a 
minor college near Boston and the other in the Smithsonian Institution.

DD
```

On Tue, 24 Nov 2009 10:30:04 -0800 (PST), Chuckles55
<chuckmoore55@gmail.com> wrote:

>Hi Everyone,
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>descriptions in as XML ?, use ISAM on Windows ?)

Have the powers that be checked on the deal of the week from IBM to
see if they can reduce costs?  Since RDz supports COBOL, there is the
possibility of moving into a development environment that might have
data dictionaries and repository support.  The general management
mentality in all too many cases has been to actively ignore advances
made in the COBOL environment and z series operating systems.  I have
serious doubts about the long term future of both COBOL and the z
series but in many cases management is not taking advantage of the
readily available tools (including Java) that are on the mainframe.
Reading about managements that refuse to upgrade from COBOL 74 makes
me wonder about their competence.
>
>Any further comments or suggestions would be appreciated,
>Chuck
```

## Thread: OT: Climate Change — 162 this month / 169 total

_Topics: [`Off-topic and spam`](topics.md#spam)_

### OT: Climate Change

- **From:** Clark F Morris <cfmpublic@ns.sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2009-12-07T16:08:05-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ihnqh51geq87k0fuooqje9vkfimqui3ct3@4ax.com>`

```
The following is a link to an article in the Wall Street Journal on
the climate change issue.  While it is skeptical, it does make some
good points about the willingness to share the raw data and the
improper location of many of the US temperature monitoring stations. A
climate change supporting article also appears in the same issue
(Monday, Dec. 7, 2009) discussing the use of gas filled tube to keep
the Alaskan permafrost cold.  This is because of the warming of the
Alaskan climate.  In many ways it is articles such as these that are
more convincing than Al Gore who reportedly has investments in ethanol
among other things.  My read of this on both sides is that there is
shilling of dubious projects (ethanol to my mind is one), ignoring of
inconvenient facts (the permafrost problem), and addiction to singling
out one cause (carbon dioxide) when various human land use decisions
(dams, deforestation, urban sprawl) may have far greater effect.


http://online.wsj.com/article_email/SB20001424052748704342404574578012533089846-lMyQjAyMDA5MDAwNzEwNDcyWj.html
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Climate Change

- **From:** Richard <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2009-12-08T12:56:31-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c1130835-1a2a-40b9-96f0-7908666a3666@z4g2000prh.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<ihnqh51geq87k0fuooqje9vkfimqui3ct3@4ax.com> <T6GdnU7t_rp-DYPWnZ2dnUVZ_rOdnZ2d@earthlink.com>`

```
On Dec 9, 6:46 am, "HeyBub" <hey...@NOSPAMgmail.com> wrote:
> Clark F Morris wrote:
> > The following is a link to an article in the Wall Street Journal on
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> were about to enter a new ice age back in the late '70s.

The change is to reflect the misconceptions that many clueless people
have that 'global warming' means that temperatures will rise
everywhere all the time. They then, for example, point to single data
point where it is cooler over a relatively small region or time and
claim somehow that this disproves 'warming'.

The actual effect of more heat in the system, apart from the obvious
general temperature rise, is that the weather will get more variable
and more extreme in many places. ie it changes even if this makes
winters colder in some places.

As for your "1970s new ice age", even the skeptics have stopped using
that clueless argument:

http://www.skepticalscience.com/ice-age-predictions-in-1970s.htm

"""1970's ice age predictions were predominantly media based with the
majority of scientific papers predicting warming.

The notion that the 1970s scientific consensus was for impending
global cooling is incorrect. In actuality, there were significantly
more papers in the 1970s predicting warming than cooling.
Scientific studies in the 1970's re global cooling

Most predictions of an impending ice age came from the popular press
(eg - Newsweek, NY Times, National Geographic, Time Magazine). As far
as peer reviewed scientific papers in the 1970s, very few papers (7 in
total) predicted global cooling. Significantly more papers (42 in
total) predicted global warming due to CO2."""

See also:

http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Global_cooling

http://www.realclimate.org/index.php/archives/2005/01/the-global-cooling-myth/
```

HeyBub wrote:
> Clark F Morris wrote:
>> The following is a link to an article in the Wall Street Journal on
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> in the late '70s.

Where I live we are said to enjoy a "moderate, sub-tropical" climate. (It 
isn't supposed to get extremely hot or cold, and, for the most part, it 
doesn't.) I have hibiscus and orchids growing outdoors and can pick oranges 
through my kitchen window... :-) My neighbours have a huge frangi-pangi tree 
and there is also some bougainvillea in the neighbourhood.

I thought you might be interested in the following, taken from a local news 
service here in Tauranga:

"Warmest spring in Tauranga

09 Dec 2009NIWA's national climate summary reports Tauranga was the warmest 
regional centre this spring, but overall spring in the Bay of Plenty was 
fairly average.
Rainfall in the Bay was normal for spring, it was sunny and temperatures 
normal and it was windy.
Temperatures in Northland, Coromandel and the Bay of Plenty were near 
normal, while there was a cooler than usual spring in most other regions.
It was sunny over much of the South Island, as well as the Waikato, Taupo, 
Bay of Plenty, Gisborne and Hawke's Bay.
Overall, the New Zealand national average temperature for spring was 11.6 
degrees Celsius, 0.4 degrees Celsius below the long-term seasonal average.
In the dry south, spring sunshine totals were more than 110 per cent of 
normal over much of the South Island, as well as throughout the Waikato, 
Taupo, Bay of Plenty, Gisborne and Hawke's Bay regions.
Of the six main centres this spring, Tauranga was the warmest, Wellington 
the wettest, Dunedin the driest and Christchurch the sunniest and coolest."

It seems to me there is material there for both sides of the debate...

I'm still personally undecided, but enjoying the weather :-)

Pete.
```

That is a fair point.  Investment based on belief makes a lot of
sense.  On the other hand, it can also constrain you from changing
that belief.  Like many other things in life, it is a bit of damned if
you either do or don't.  Even if climate change is real AND
attributable to carbon dioxide in the atmosphere (and I'm on the fence
and more worried about carbonic acid in the ocean), ethanol may just
exacerbate the problem.  He may have the right problem and wrong
solution.  

Someone else commented that if the climate change people were serious,
instead of having the conference with everybody going to Copenhagen,
they should have made it a world wide video conference with everybody
attending from home.  I liked the idea at first but on second thought
I doubt the technology is there yet.

Clark F Morris wrote:
> On Wed, 9 Dec 2009 12:54:27 +1300, "Pete Dashwood"
> <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[36 more quoted lines elided]…
> I doubt the technology is there yet.

The technology is definitely there, and has been for some time. It recently 
improved...

I video conference at least once a week and often 3 or 4 times to the US, 
Canada, Australia, Europe, and New Zealand.Mostly it is with a single other 
person, but, on occasion I have participated in up to 5 people and it was 
fine.

When I first used Skype it had sound only and conversations consisted of 
jerky sentences with clipped words. There were usually around 2 million 
people using it at any given moment. When video first became available it 
was also jerky and poor quality. By then there were around 4 million people 
using it at any given moment. Today, both sound and vision are excellent and 
most times I am online with it it is showing around 11 million users. They 
have released an "industrial strength" version.

Skype is free. You need a webcam or video and a sound card. Wecams are 
available for around $20. I see no reason why anyone can't be online with 
sound and picture.

>>
>>>  My read of this on both sides is that there is
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>>> http://online.wsj.com/article_email/SB20001424052748704342404574578012533089846-lMyQjAyMDA5MDAwNzEwNDcyWj.html

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Climate Change

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2009-12-09T10:10:38-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<k5mvh55ncgnvf2guptqo5svk3o9qmncmpr@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<ihnqh51geq87k0fuooqje9vkfimqui3ct3@4ax.com> <7o879kF2sqjjlU1@mid.individual.net> <f73uh5p5e7gllba48bdobhgndhe44n5c4a@4ax.com>`

```
On Tue, 08 Dec 2009 22:43:38 -0400, Clark F Morris
<cfmpublic@ns.sympatico.ca> wrote:

>Even if climate change is real AND
>attributable to carbon dioxide in the atmosphere (and I'm on the fence
>and more worried about carbonic acid in the ocean), ethanol may just
>exacerbate the problem.  He may have the right problem and wrong
>solution

I've seen arguments that climate warming started with irrigation when
irrigation started, and grew when larger portions of the world were
irrigated.

But stopping irrigation is an unacceptable option, so people go on to
find something they can choose that isn't so expensive.

The biggest pollutants may be roads, buildings, and farms with dams
close behind.     I say "may", because it is possible that
over-fishing the oceans is bigger - but we haven't established that
for sure.   (And there is the variable pollutant of war).

Trouble is - none of these are solvable without population control,
and not many parts of the world are ready for that.   So we target
areas that we can target, whether they are effective or not. (Ignoring
the elephant in the room).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Climate Change

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2009-12-14T09:47:20+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ol26qF3qc6ehU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<ihnqh51geq87k0fuooqje9vkfimqui3ct3@4ax.com> <854fc499-e8c3-4a96-8563-c97549b7b311@m38g2000yqd.googlegroups.com> <7oes1sF3pjpheU1@mid.individual.net> <6eac6526-8adc-4741-9b29-6bf4885a0a2d@a32g2000yqm.googlegroups.com> <hg3bcd$e8n$1@reader1.panix.com>`

```
docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
> In article
> <6eac6526-8adc-4741-9b29-6bf4885a0a2d@a32g2000yqm.googlegroups.com>,
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> DD

Of course, chaqu'un a son gout...

But there are some delightful local varieties of clam and cockle here which 
are really delicious.  One of my favourites is called "tua-tua". It is 
pleasant to find an area of deserted beach, take a small bucket and collect 
a few dozen of these. Bring them home and hose the contents of the bucket to 
remove excess sand. Place a knob of butter in a saucepan with some crushed 
garlic and heat it til the butter has melted, then add half a cup of water 
and a cup of dry white wine and bring to the boil. Put the tua-tuas into a 
steamer on top of the saucepan and let it simmer. The tua-tuas start to open 
as the steam heats them and within about 5 to 10 minutes they are done. (You 
can tell because they are all open; any that aren't can be discarded). Grind 
fresh black pepper over them and eat them directly off the shell.

This is a simply amazing snack while watching TV or just sitting outside 
with a glass of appropriately chilled Sauvignon Blanc or Chardonnay (both of 
which are cheap, of good quality, and plentiful here). Personally, I prefer 
red wines but I have to admit these are very good with seafood...

In addition to the large green lipped mussels (which are reputed to have 
anti-carcinogenic properties), supermarkets here have recently taken to 
stocking South Island clams. (These are presented in specially constructed 
stainless steel trays which have seawater circulated and sprayed over them 
so the shellfish are are alive at the time you buy them.) I was agreeably 
surprised at how cheap they are... a kilo of fresh, large, green lipped 
mussels for under $4...(mind you, they rip us off on everything else so I 
guess it cancels out... :-))

I really hate the idea that these seafood stocks could be at risk due to 
Climate Change and I sincerely hope the people who are persuaded of it are 
wrong. I'm inclined to believe that lack of conservation, management, and 
over-fishing are much more serious and immediate threats, but that doesn't 
diminish the threat from Climate Change, if it is real.

Pete.
```

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2009-12-16T09:02:58+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7oq8bjF3qp51dU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<ihnqh51geq87k0fuooqje9vkfimqui3ct3@4ax.com> <854fc499-e8c3-4a96-8563-c97549b7b311@m38g2000yqd.googlegroups.com> <7oes1sF3pjpheU1@mid.individual.net> <6eac6526-8adc-4741-9b29-6bf4885a0a2d@a32g2000yqm.googlegroups.com> <hg3bcd$e8n$1@reader1.panix.com> <7ol26qF3qc6ehU1@mid.individual.net> <ec433a86-f967-45b1-9ea1-58fbf01d0b00@g26g2000yqe.googlegroups.com>`

Alistair wrote:
> On Dec 13, 8:47 pm, "Pete Dashwood"
> <dashw...@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[61 more quoted lines elided]…
> tv)?

Ah, Alistair, there is a significant difference in the subtlety of steaming 
as opposed to immersion.  The steam is hotter than the liquid which means 
the shellfish open quicker and their juices join the liquid which recycles 
as steam. They cook without having their own delicate flavour overwhelmed by 
the flavours of the liquid, although those flavours are present. The bottom 
line is: they taste better. (Don't believe everything you see on TV... :-)

I never waste the liquid, recycling it into fish stock or using it as a base 
for seafood pastas.

As for the extra pot... I have a dishwasher that does pots as well...

You probably gathered I enjoy good food... and cooking it... :-)

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ OT: it isn't always the Humans... WAS Re: Climate Change

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2009-12-18T14:06:03+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7p02ruF9jrU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<ihnqh51geq87k0fuooqje9vkfimqui3ct3@4ax.com> <854fc499-e8c3-4a96-8563-c97549b7b311@m38g2000yqd.googlegroups.com> <7oes1sF3pjpheU1@mid.individual.net> <6eac6526-8adc-4741-9b29-6bf4885a0a2d@a32g2000yqm.googlegroups.com> <hg3bcd$e8n$1@reader1.panix.com> <7ol26qF3qc6ehU1@mid.individual.net> <ec433a86-f967-45b1-9ea1-58fbf01d0b00@g26g2000yqe.googlegroups.com>`

```
Alistair wrote:
> On Dec 13, 8:47 pm, "Pete Dashwood"
> <dashw...@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[61 more quoted lines elided]…
> tv)?

Sometimes, it isn't the Humans who cause a problem. Occasionally, Nature 
decides it has had enough or a series of conditions combine to cause an 
unusual event.

Local shellfish are going to be off the menu here for the next 6 weeks or so 
(maybe longer). This has nothing to do with industrial emissions (there 
aren't any), discharging effluent into the ocean (we generally don't do 
that - sewage gets treated before being put into the ocean) , or dumping of 
toxic wastes (we don't have any). It is simply a combination of climatic 
factors leading to an explosion of algae...

Our shops will be selling shellfish from other parts of the country, but it 
is still a pity we can't simply go and get some...

http://www.sunlive.co.nz/11304a1.page

Pete.
```

- **References:** `<ihnqh51geq87k0fuooqje9vkfimqui3ct3@4ax.com> <7ol26qF3qc6ehU1@mid.individual.net> <ec433a86-f967-45b1-9ea1-58fbf01d0b00@g26g2000yqe.googlegroups.com> <7p02ruF9jrU1@mid.individual.net>`

```
In article <7p02ruF9jrU1@mid.individual.net>,
Pete Dashwood <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:

[snip]

>Sometimes, it isn't the Humans who cause a problem. Occasionally, Nature 
>decides it has had enough or a series of conditions combine to cause an 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>factors leading to an explosion of algae...

I recall being taught that one should not consume shellfish in months 
whose names do not contain an 'r'... March, fine, April, fine, May, no, 
June, no... all the way to September, when it turns fine.

Of course... this assumes a particluar calendar and naming-convention; I 
wonder what rules might be applied to other calendars in the same 
geographic area.

DD
```

- **From:** Alistair <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<ihnqh51geq87k0fuooqje9vkfimqui3ct3@4ax.com> <7ol26qF3qc6ehU1@mid.individual.net> <ec433a86-f967-45b1-9ea1-58fbf01d0b00@g26g2000yqe.googlegroups.com> <7p02ruF9jrU1@mid.individual.net> <hgiq7r$r4c$1@reader1.panix.com>`

On Dec 19, 3:07 pm, docdw...@panix.com () wrote:
> In article <7p02ruF9j...@mid.individual.net>,
>
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
> DD

Where algal blooms occur (usually twice a year, in the Spring and
Autumn) it may be wise to not eat shellfish. Also, where shellfish
have a certain breeding season it may be wise not to eat them around
that time (before, during and immediately after as they would need
time to recover).

Did anyone tell you why you shouldn't eat them at that time?
```

> In article <37ce65bc-0e1b-4928-8587-fb998e6dd...@e27g2000yqd.googlegroups.com>,
>
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
> DD

Just done a google search. Apparently the American Indians started the
don't eat shellfish when there is an R in the month, possibly as a
result of rapid detioration of the harvested animals and possibly
because shellfish concentrate the algal bloom toxins. The spawning bit
refers to oysters which apparently can be unpleasant in flavour when
spawning. Genetic modification has been applied to oysters to prevent
spawning which begs the question about how exactly do they reproduce?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: it isn't always the Humans... WAS Re: Climate Change

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2009-12-23T01:21:00+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7pbrtdFrdkU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<ihnqh51geq87k0fuooqje9vkfimqui3ct3@4ax.com> <7p02ruF9jrU1@mid.individual.net> <hgiq7r$r4c$1@reader1.panix.com> <37ce65bc-0e1b-4928-8587-fb998e6ddbd0@e27g2000yqd.googlegroups.com> <hglk47$a16$1@reader1.panix.com> <428e0f20-b90c-480e-9399-e273a1710701@21g2000yqj.googlegroups.com>`

```
Alistair wrote:
> On Dec 20, 4:41 pm, docdw...@panix.com () wrote:
>> In article
…[34 more quoted lines elided]…
> don't eat shellfish when there is an R in the month,

Huh?! That makes no sense at all. American Indians didn't use the Roman 
calendar. And they weren't likely to have assimilated a western calendar 
until at least the 18th century.

This old adage goes way back before the 17th century when Henry Buttes made 
a reference to it in 1599, in Dyets drie dinner:
  "The oyster is unseasonable and unwholesome in all months that have not 
the letter R in their name".

We know today that this is an Old Wive's Tale. I once lived on an island for 
the best part of a year and my diet consisted mainly of oysters, with which 
the shoreline rocks were covered, along with fish I caught, vegetables I 
grew and the odd barbeque leftovers from passing yachtspeople.

Oysters are good to eat all year round.

> possibly as a
> result of rapid detioration of the harvested animals and possibly
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> spawning which begs the question about how exactly do they reproduce?

The genetically modified ones don't...

Do you know what kind of noise annoys an oyster?

A noisy noise annoys an oyster.

Pete.
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7pe9emFdfeU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<ihnqh51geq87k0fuooqje9vkfimqui3ct3@4ax.com> <7p02ruF9jrU1@mid.individual.net> <hgiq7r$r4c$1@reader1.panix.com> <37ce65bc-0e1b-4928-8587-fb998e6ddbd0@e27g2000yqd.googlegroups.com> <hglk47$a16$1@reader1.panix.com> <428e0f20-b90c-480e-9399-e273a1710701@21g2000yqj.googlegroups.com> <7pbrtdFrdkU1@mid.individual.net> <32a149e7-ba1d-436a-9852-40edd75437ca@b32g2000yqd.googlegroups.com> <7pcv6bF8meU1@mid.individual.net> <a8c35417-4ba0-4c94-977c-55cbafc67f31@u8g2000prd.googlegroups.com>`

```
Richard wrote:
> On Dec 23, 11:23 am, "Pete Dashwood"
> <dashw...@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[87 more quoted lines elided]…
> a 7 year old ?

"Except ye become as one of these, ye shall not enter the Kingdom of 
Heaven..."

I can still remember the very first joke I ever heard... I was three years 
old and already reading books, thanks to parents who both spent time with me 
and instilled in me the yearning for learning. (No TV... how did we ever 
survive...?)
"A little worm poked his head up out of the ground, sighed, and looked 
around. A moment later another little worm popped up close to the first. The 
first worm looked at the second worm, realised she was female and said: 'I 
love you, will you marry me?'
The second worm smiled and said: 'Don't be a fool, I'm your other end!'

Although the hermaphrodite nature of the earthworm was unknown to me at that 
time, the joke still works at a different level.

I am grateful to my father for imparting in me a love and respect for humour 
in all its varied forms and across cultural and age boundaries. I have found 
myself in numerous situations where humour has been a valuable instrument to 
lessen tension and anxiety. (And anyway, who doesn't enjoy a laugh 
occasionally...?)

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Climate Change

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "HeyBub" <heybub@NOSPAMgmail.com>
- **Date:** 2009-12-11T09:44:22-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<VMOdneiD0KlK9b_WnZ2dnUVZ_sudnZ2d@earthlink.com>`
- **References:** `<ihnqh51geq87k0fuooqje9vkfimqui3ct3@4ax.com> <7o879kF2sqjjlU1@mid.individual.net> <f73uh5p5e7gllba48bdobhgndhe44n5c4a@4ax.com> <k5mvh55ncgnvf2guptqo5svk3o9qmncmpr@4ax.com> <854fc499-e8c3-4a96-8563-c97549b7b311@m38g2000yqd.googlegroups.com>`

Alistair wrote:
>>
> You mean that you haven't been force-fed any studies by marine
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Of course not! When we get down to three crabs and five oysters, they'll be 
declared "endangered" and the harvesting will stop. We'll then move on to 
eating prawn-flavored tofu. Or sea plankton.

In reality what will happen is that ocean fauna will get more and more 
expensive as they become more scarce. The market will then equilibrate. Just 
like it has with, oh, caviar. There will come a time when there will be no 
shortage of Alaskan Crab Legs, just a shortage of CHEAP Alaskan Crab Legs. I 
say let the market make the decisions.

>
>
>  (And there is the variable pollutant of war).

War is good. It cuts down on the number of people who want to eat shrimp.
```
