###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Climate Change

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2009-12-14T10:00:52+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ol305F3o6e8sU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<ihnqh51geq87k0fuooqje9vkfimqui3ct3@4ax.com> <7o879kF2sqjjlU1@mid.individual.net> <f73uh5p5e7gllba48bdobhgndhe44n5c4a@4ax.com> <k5mvh55ncgnvf2guptqo5svk3o9qmncmpr@4ax.com> <854fc499-e8c3-4a96-8563-c97549b7b311@m38g2000yqd.googlegroups.com> <VMOdneiD0KlK9b_WnZ2dnUVZ_sudnZ2d@earthlink.com> <3ef04452-2859-453d-985e-48ad03609e16@l13g2000yqb.googlegroups.com>`

```
Alistair wrote:
> On Dec 11, 3:44 pm, "HeyBub" <hey...@NOSPAMgmail.com> wrote:
>> Alistair wrote:
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…
> managed. I don't understand what the fuss is with caviar.

I was amazed to find in Prague, in the late 1980s (before the wall came 
down) that a tin of beluga caviar which would have sold in London for around 
500 UK pounds at that time, could be obtained for DM 100 (around 35 UK 
pounds at the time). This may have been an illegal transaction where the 
money was simply pocketed; I don't know, but it is the only time in my life 
when I felt that caviar was affordable... :-)

I think the "fuss" is that (like oysters and other shellfish) the flavour of 
caviar is unique. There isn't anything else that tastes like it and it is 
strong (unlike the delicate flavour of lobster, for example). Certainly, 
hard boiled eggs seem to complement it well and it is a pleasant enough 
snack if the price is right. Personally I wouldn't pay the going price, even 
if I could afford to, but the diversity of our world is such that there are 
other people who certainly can and will pay for it.

I don't think the quality of my life suffers because I don't have 
caviar...but, I do start worry about what I am doing wrong if I don't get 
lobster at least ONCE a year... :-)

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Climate Change

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()
- **Date:** 2009-12-14T16:47:59+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<hg5q7v$gc8$1@reader1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<ihnqh51geq87k0fuooqje9vkfimqui3ct3@4ax.com> <3ef04452-2859-453d-985e-48ad03609e16@l13g2000yqb.googlegroups.com> <7ol305F3o6e8sU1@mid.individual.net> <993e08f0-bdaf-47bb-b9c8-7ce6cb9bbe9e@d20g2000yqh.googlegroups.com>`

```
In article <993e08f0-bdaf-47bb-b9c8-7ce6cb9bbe9e@d20g2000yqh.googlegroups.com>,
Alistair  <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
>On Dec 13, 9:00?pm, "Pete Dashwood"
><dashw...@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:

[snip]

>> I don't think the quality of my life suffers because I don't have
>> caviar...but, I do start worry about what I am doing wrong if I don't get
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>Never eaten lobster.

Now we fall into the realm of the marine biologist... I was taught, long 
ago and by United States Americans, that a real 'lobster' is an arthropod 
with different-sized and different-function'd major ('great') claws, a 
shorter one for grabbing and hold, a longer one for fighting and tearing.

'South African' lobsters did not have this assymmetry and were (again, as 
I was taught by United States Americans), quite delicious to eat... but 
not Real Lobsters, more like Oversized Marine Crayfish.

As for never having eaten it... we all were virgins once, Mr Maclean, and 
it is a Wonderful World that has in it new foods to try.  I don't know 
what the situation in Auld Bloighty is nowadays but in the United States 
one can, in many places, walk up to the fish-counter in a Major 
Supermarket, select a live lobster from a tank of the beasties (I'd 
suggest your getting a barely-legal, or 'chicken lobster', weighing 
between .75 and 1.25 pounds) and for no extra charge it will be steamed 
for you on the spot.

I would also suggest dealing with the eating as a kind of dissection, 
where every joint needs to be dislocated and every plate of body-armor 
removed.  *Everything* except the shell and the stomach (a lumpish sac in 
which you can feel three 'molars') is not only edible but of a different 
texture and tase; claw meat is not tail meat is not the crunchiness of 
the gills is not the rich fullness of the tomalo.

And... if you don't like it, so what?  Make a face, spit it out and go 
back to bangers 'n mash, none the worse for the wear and now able to speak 
from experience.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Climate Change

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2009-12-14T11:27:55-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<pf0di5he9hak6rk14evr8o2ks2abq54nlv@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<ihnqh51geq87k0fuooqje9vkfimqui3ct3@4ax.com> <3ef04452-2859-453d-985e-48ad03609e16@l13g2000yqb.googlegroups.com> <7ol305F3o6e8sU1@mid.individual.net> <993e08f0-bdaf-47bb-b9c8-7ce6cb9bbe9e@d20g2000yqh.googlegroups.com> <hg5q7v$gc8$1@reader1.panix.com>`

```
I just read that one expected consequence of global warming will be
larger lobsters.

I suppose this is if over fishing doesn't continue the process of
smaller *everything*.

One thing that appears to be happening with the Internet.    With it
very easy to be published (in an HTML document), it seems that for
many, all statements have equal value.    Sarah Palin is as much of an
expert as anybody else, at least for people who agree with her
conclusions.

In fact, the old "experts" (scientists) have enough disagreement among
themselves that their opinions count less than those who don't need
facts to confuse their conclusions.
```

```
> I just read that one expected consequence of global warming will be
> larger lobsters.

Don't know about larger but their shells/carapaces will probably be
thicker. Lobsters are one of the few species identified, so far, that
will benefit from inceased CO2 entering the oceans and acidifying the
water.
```

```
Pete.
```

```
On Thu, 17 Dec 2009 05:51:43 -0800 (PST), Alistair
<alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:

>Eating roasted Floppsy bunny is a lot easier when it has been
>dissected before hitting the plate and doesn't resemble the original
>whole animal.  Ditto for chickens although I still balk at the Parsons

I don't get my meat from animals, nor my bananas from banana trees - I
get mine from grocery stores.

Isaac Asimov once wrote a story - I believe it was "A Matter of
Taste".      In the story, this guy won a cooking contest until they
found out his secret ingredient.    He had been on a trip to Earth and
picked up some garlic.   Which had been alive - and touched dirt! How
disgusting!
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Climate Change

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2009-12-17T11:50:11+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ot6h6F3rhj8gU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<ihnqh51geq87k0fuooqje9vkfimqui3ct3@4ax.com> <3ef04452-2859-453d-985e-48ad03609e16@l13g2000yqb.googlegroups.com> <7ol305F3o6e8sU1@mid.individual.net> <993e08f0-bdaf-47bb-b9c8-7ce6cb9bbe9e@d20g2000yqh.googlegroups.com> <hg5q7v$gc8$1@reader1.panix.com> <535db1bc-e599-445d-9921-af2c2ad964d6@g31g2000vbr.googlegroups.com>`

```
Alistair wrote:
> On Dec 14, 4:47 pm, docdw...@panix.com () wrote:
>> In article
…[62 more quoted lines elided]…
> whole animal from the contents of my dinner plate.

That raises a very interesting point.

Many people have the same problem when it comes to food; it seems to be the 
nature of Human Beings to anthropomorphize...

A few nights ago I took a lady out for dinner to an excellent French 
restaurant we are fortunate to have here. She made the fatal mistake of 
asking me to order for her... (She really doesn't know me very well but she 
is learning fast :-))

I ordered escargot and she recoiled in horror...

"Snails! I can't eat snails...!"

"Well, you entered a verbal contract so now we find out whether your word is 
good or not..."

I could see the wheels turning as she looked for escape with honour.  There 
was none, but I decided to cut her some slack.

"OK, think about this... You eat lamb, right? You drive around New Zealand 
in September and see delightful little creatures gambolling in the fields. 
You laugh and are delighted, and yet a part of you knows that they will be 
on the table by Christmas. Come Christmas and people at the table are 
passing the mint sauce and commenting on how delicious the lamb is. Is your 
experience diminished by having seen lambs gambolling? If it is, then there 
are a few things you need to get straight. We have no moral obligation to 
not kill animals we raise for food. (We DO have a moral obligation to ensure 
they have happy lives and are not treated cruelly or inhumanely.) When Larry 
the lamb is gambolling in the pasture (or playing with the kids in the back 
yard as a pet) he is a "lamb". We can enjoy his company and interact with 
him.But when he's on your plate, he's  "food". A process has occurred by 
which Larry ceased to be, and food was created. This process is inevitable 
for all living things, including us, and is a natural part of life. You are 
NOT eating Larry; Larry is long gone. You are eating food that you have 
teeth designed to assist you in eating.

Now let's think about the snails. You have an image in your head of slimy, 
unpleasant villains that eat the lettuces while you sleep. This may or may 
not be true. It doesn't matter. When they are cooked in their shells with 
garlic butter and parsley they are simply "food".  Delicious food. (Besides, 
the snails we are going to eat are not the common garden variety, they are 
raised especially for eating.)

Think about it in the few remaining minutes before they bring the snails. If 
you decide you really can't manage them, you are released from your 
contract, and I'll eat them... :-)"

She took a swig of wine and when the snails arrived she not only ate them 
but thoroughly enjoyed them. (I like opening up new worlds for people... 
:-))

The next day she commented to me how unexpectedly delicious they were.

"From now on, I'll think of them as 'land prawns'..."

It is important that we dont anthropomorphize food; just enjoy it... :-)

Pete.
```

```
Alistair wrote:
> On Dec 13, 9:00 pm, "Pete Dashwood"
> <dashw...@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[63 more quoted lines elided]…
> side.

I never heard about that. 1400 miles seems a long way for a lobster (or 
anyone...) to walk. :-)

 The place in NZ which is best known for crayfish is called Kaikoura 
(kai=food, koura=lobster), which is also a base for whale watching. It is on 
the West coast of the South Island.

The local variety is a crayfish which does not have the large claws of a 
Maine lobster, however, the body is usually larger and the flesh tastes 
almost the same. There is a subtle difference but I enjoy both equally.

When I was a teenager we used to go snorkelling around rocks in certain 
places and could usually pick up a couple of  rock lobster, which we cooked 
on the beach in seawater. Those days are gone, although a local fisherman 
was telling me the crayfish are making a reappearance and they are plentiful 
this year.

Currently, they sell here for $100 a kilo which puts them way beyond most 
people's budget. (Like I said, ONCE a year for me... :-)) Almost the entire 
catch goes to China and Japan and that is why the price is so high. Every so 
often (although not as often as I would lke... :-)) somebody will give me 
one... There are many people here who scuba dive or know somebody who 
does...

Never mind. I guess if we could have it every day, it wouldn't be a treat... 
:-)

>
> Never eaten lobster.

Like most seafood, there is much in the preparation. Newburg (with a brandy 
sauce) and Thermidor (with a mustard sauce) are the two most popular ways of 
cooking lobster, but personally, I like it chargrilled with butter. 
(Actually, I like it any way it is cooked, as long as it isn't over or under 
done... :-)) I have also microwaved them and the results are delicious as 
they literally cook in their own juices in the shell. (I hasten to add I 
sedate or kill them first... the process takes around 7 minutes and I like 
to think they never know what happened.) You can sedate them by putting them 
in the freezer for 40 minutes or so. Their processes slow right down and 
they go into a coma-like state. You can kill them by piercing the brain.

Pete.
```

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2009-12-18T13:56:37+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7p02a8F7apU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<ihnqh51geq87k0fuooqje9vkfimqui3ct3@4ax.com> <7o879kF2sqjjlU1@mid.individual.net> <f73uh5p5e7gllba48bdobhgndhe44n5c4a@4ax.com> <k5mvh55ncgnvf2guptqo5svk3o9qmncmpr@4ax.com> <854fc499-e8c3-4a96-8563-c97549b7b311@m38g2000yqd.googlegroups.com> <VMOdneiD0KlK9b_WnZ2dnUVZ_sudnZ2d@earthlink.com> <3ef04452-2859-453d-985e-48ad03609e16@l13g2000yqb.googlegroups.com> <7ol305F3o6e8sU1@mid.individual.net> <993e08f0-bdaf-47bb-b9c8-7ce6cb9bbe9e@d20g2000yqh.googlegroups.com> <vfkki5pootdoj8i5lng7b8k7oe4hhm98ga@4ax.com>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
> Once a bureaucracy gets started, it survives without making sense. We
> see it with programming and standards.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> http://www.hlswatch.com/2009/10/15/%E2%80%9Cdo-i-have-the-right-to-refuse-this-search%E2%80%9D/


A very interesting and well written article.

More and more it seems that the old adage is simply true:

"You can't buck the system".

I thought about this for a while after I read it.

I can't see a solution. You have to recruit people to do the screening and 
they are not all going to be the sharpest tools in the box. You can train 
them and insist that they apply the stated policies, but then, as she points 
out, compliance becomes more important than common sense.

I believe the fact is that it is IMPOSSIBLE to implement an effective 
screening process against determined terrorists and the best we can do is 
"make it difficult" for them. However, the general public require better 
than that, so the paraphernalia and processes of a "security process" are 
implemented, as much to reassure travellers as to actually catch terrorists.

Intelligence (in all senses of the word) remains the best weapon in the 
fight against terrorism.

Pete.
```

```
were true... :-)
```

```
On Fri, 18 Dec 2009 13:56:37 +1300, "Pete Dashwood"
<dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:

>I can't see a solution. You have to recruit people to do the screening and 
>they are not all going to be the sharpest tools in the box. You can train 
>them and insist that they apply the stated policies, but then, as she points 
>out, compliance becomes more important than common sense.

The 9-11 solution was pretty much decided and implemented over the
skies of Pennsylvania on 9-11.

The U.S. spends less than 1% per train passenger security than for air
traffic security.    I suspect that's not at all the same in Spain or
Japan.     This kind of security is politically reactive "do
something", as opposed to analyzed for costs and benefits.

But then, even programming assignments can be guilty of this (at a
smaller cost).
```

```
On Mon, 14 Dec 2009 14:05:59 -0500, SkippyPB
<swiegand@Nospam.neo.rr.com> wrote:

>There is nothing that can be harvested from a whale that can't be made
>synthetically including whale oil.  There is no need for large scale
>whaling.  The Eskimos in Alaska are permitted to kill enough whales to
>feed their villages.  This they do and they don't kill too many and
>they use every bit.  That is sensible use of this "resource".

I'm don't see that using everything is a sufficient criterion to make
use of a resource sensible.   (And they don't use everything).

It's funny that when we kill an animal and use its skin to make a belt
or shoes, people aren't as upset if we scrape the hair off and throw
it away.   Leather's OK, fur isn't.    It isn't waste that people
complain about.

You put quotes around "resource".   I infer that you see something
about whales that disqualify them as being a resource the same way as
trees, cows, or engineers are resources.
```

```
On 2009-12-07 14:08:05 -0600, Clark F Morris <cfmpublic@ns.sympatico.ca> said:

…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Climate Change

- **From:** Richard <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2009-12-08T21:13:27-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3d6bcf03-3492-4247-a932-0356e9dea2b7@m33g2000pri.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<ihnqh51geq87k0fuooqje9vkfimqui3ct3@4ax.com> <4b1f24f0$0$5340$bbae4d71@news.suddenlink.net>`

```
On Dec 9, 5:17 pm, Paul <paul-nospamatall.rauler...@mac.com> wrote:
> On 2009-12-07 14:08:05 -0600, Clark F Morris <cfmpub...@ns.sympatico.ca> said:
>
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
> simply by painting roofs white.

What percentage of the earth's surface is house roofs ?

My calculation for NZ is less than 0.1%. Given that land area is 30%
of total surface then overall you are talking about less than 0.0003
of the surface. The change of albedo would probably be less than the
effects of manufacturing, transporting and applying the paint,
especially if it was hydrocarbon (oil) based.

In any case half the time the surface is radiating heat out into space
so if they were white they would be less efficient radiators.

Average result: None at all.


> There is probably something shady going on, but I don't have the
> resources to figure out what that something is.

Just keep repeating "D'oh".

> Someone needs to just follow the money.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Climate Change

- **From:** Paul <paul-nospamatall.raulerson@mac.com>
- **Date:** 2009-12-09T00:59:19-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4b1f4ac7$0$5318$bbae4d71@news.suddenlink.net>`
- **References:** `<ihnqh51geq87k0fuooqje9vkfimqui3ct3@4ax.com> <4b1f24f0$0$5340$bbae4d71@news.suddenlink.net> <3d6bcf03-3492-4247-a932-0356e9dea2b7@m33g2000pri.googlegroups.com>`

```
On 2009-12-08 23:13:27 -0600, Richard <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> said:

> On Dec 9, 5:17ï¿½pm, Paul <paul-nospamatall.rauler...@mac.com> wrote:
>> On 2009-12-07 14:08:05 -0600, Clark F Morris <cfmpub...@ns.sympatico.ca>
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
> What percentage of the earth's surface is house roofs ?

You need to take into account that a vast majority of the roofs in 
developed countries are dark, and present significant heat sinks -
*not* coincidentally. (It's a significant factor in most urban planning 
studies.)  Your calculation of the effect is way off too, in fact, your 
calculation leaves out so many factors, that it is not really 
meaningful.

And this is just ONE of the factors that needs to be calculated with 
precision to determine not only if Global Warming is happening,
but what effect, if any, it is having on the planetary climate. There 
are hundreds, if not thousands of similar factors.

And that is before you consider the really chaotic factors, which have 
never been calculated, or even measured, with precision. Global wind 
patterns, atmospheric composition, and so forth. Lots and lots of 
things.

We should be spending time and money on doing those measurements and 
calculations, and
not on Hollywood Hysteria.

-Paul
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Climate Change

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Richard <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2009-12-09T10:02:09-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<266587d7-3804-4c55-a6a1-2b0a53e4e3f4@b36g2000prf.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<ihnqh51geq87k0fuooqje9vkfimqui3ct3@4ax.com> <4b1f24f0$0$5340$bbae4d71@news.suddenlink.net> <3d6bcf03-3492-4247-a932-0356e9dea2b7@m33g2000pri.googlegroups.com> <4b1f4ac7$0$5318$bbae4d71@news.suddenlink.net>`

```
On Dec 9, 7:59 pm, Paul <paul-nospamatall.rauler...@mac.com> wrote:
> On 2009-12-08 23:13:27 -0600, Richard <rip...@Azonic.co.nz> said:
>
…[33 more quoted lines elided]…
> developed countries are dark, and present significant heat sinks -

In order to dispel the idea that your research has been limited to
your keyboard please give a reference to a survey that shows that. A
sampling with Google Earth does not show any such conclusion.

And, as I said, increasing the reflectivity during the day will reduce
the heat radiation at night reducing your claimed effect overall.

> *not* coincidentally. (It's a significant factor in most urban planning
> studies.)  Your calculation of the effect is way off too, in fact, your
> calculation leaves out so many factors, that it is not really
> meaningful.

My only 'calculation' was an estimate of the area of roofs compared to
the surface area for NZ. How is that "way off" ?

As for being "meaningful" all that I see from your side is vauge
claims and hand waving with no support at all.



> And this is just ONE of the factors that needs to be calculated with
> precision to determine not only if Global Warming is happening,
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> not on Hollywood Hysteria.

The only "Hollywood Hysteria" is when someone makes a stupid
unsupported claim such as: "a significant change in the planetary
albedo could be accomplished
simply by painting roofs white".
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Climate Change

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Paul <paul-nospamatall.raulerson@mac.com>
- **Date:** 2009-12-10T00:13:57-06:00

```
On Thu, 10 Dec 2009 09:14:59 -0800 (PST), Richard
<riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>On Dec 10, 7:13ï¿½pm, Paul <paul-nospamatall.rauler...@mac.com> wrote:
>> On 2009-12-09 12:02:09 -0600, Richard <rip...@Azonic.co.nz> said:
…[124 more quoted lines elided]…
>

It was the US Energy Secretary Steven Chu who suggested this painting
of roofs (and roads and other things) white to reflect the sunlight
and offset CO2 emissions.  He based his statement on a presentation he
had seen given by Hashem Akbari of Lawrence Berkeley National Lab.

The real discussion shouldn't be whether this is done nor not but
whether it is as effective as implied.  My own feeling is yes it
should be done in concert with a lot of other things to reduce CO2
emissions.  By itself, it would not be effective enough.  Here is a
reasonable discussion with links to scientific and plain English
explanations.  It is presented by Roy W. Spencer, Ph. D.,
climatologist and a former NASA scientist.

http://www.drroyspencer.com/2009/05/white-roofs-and-global-warming-a-more-realistic-perspective/

Regards,
```

```
"""Q.  Did Chuck Yeager have the right stuff in order to become an
astronaut?

Answer

Yes, but he wasn't interested or eligible to apply for the astronaut
program. He wasn't eligible due to not having a college degree and he
wasn't interested because the seven Mercury astronauts weren't
selected based on their skills as pilots, they were selected based on
their ability to withstand what American scientists guessed were the
extreme physical conditions that would be encountered during space
flight. Because the astronauts wouldn't really be flying the space
craft (which was designed to be flown automatically and remotely from
Mission Control), Yeager, as America's hottest test pilot, did not
want to become "Spam in a can" as he called the astronauts sitting in
their capsule."""


What point are you attempting to make ?


> bill
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Climate Change

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Richard <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2009-12-10T11:48:07-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1c6207e5-519a-4248-a575-d67f7b324e40@h40g2000prf.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<ihnqh51geq87k0fuooqje9vkfimqui3ct3@4ax.com> <4b1f24f0$0$5340$bbae4d71@news.suddenlink.net> <3d6bcf03-3492-4247-a932-0356e9dea2b7@m33g2000pri.googlegroups.com> <4b1f4ac7$0$5318$bbae4d71@news.suddenlink.net> <266587d7-3804-4c55-a6a1-2b0a53e4e3f4@b36g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <4b2091a5$0$5322$bbae4d71@news.suddenlink.net> <40893b3c-a973-430b-b983-e4fafd2d18fa@a10g2000pre.googlegroups.com> <stg2i5tf54j4mobdhq2iqpdounuu1v9ogs@4ax.com>`

```
On Dec 11, 8:01 am, SkippyPB <swieg...@Nospam.neo.rr.com> wrote:
> On Thu, 10 Dec 2009 09:14:59 -0800 (PST), Richard
>
…[127 more quoted lines elided]…
> had seen given by Hashem Akbari of Lawrence Berkeley National Lab.

Which is rather different from what Paul claimed for 'roofs' alone.

And while increasing the reflection _may_ reduce by a tiny amount the
heating effect of the sun during the day it will also reduce, by a
similar factor, the loss of heat by radiation at night.

(Something that Paul apparently fails to understand).

As it happens this effect is a good thing for many buildings,
especially in hot climates, which is why so many already have bright
roofs where building materials allow.

Sydney, for example, is known as "the city of red roofs", particularly
around the airport, as can be understood if you fly there. That is
because clay tiles are cheap and need no maintenance, but few of them
are 'dark' and many roofs there are bright metal - usually aluminium
paint.


> The real discussion shouldn't be whether this is done nor not but
> whether it is as effective as implied.  My own feeling is yes it
> should be done in concert with a lot of other things to reduce CO2
> emissions.  By itself, it would not be effective enough.  

Exactly. Painting roads is simply undoable, they cease to be usable
roads. (oh wait, that would work in reducing CO2).


> Here is a
> reasonable discussion with links to scientific and plain English
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Note to Paul: _That_ is a link, the sort which provides support to an
argument. If you could provide these, though of course not this one
which destroys your claims, then you would be doing more than just
hand waving and crying 'wrong'.




> Regards,
> --
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> Steve
```

```
answered his questions.

that nonsense for what, around a decade now?

or even interest in a subject.

everything are contentious,  erroneous, pretentious, and certainly

that. Sometimes
I  do take my born-and-raised-in-backwoods-Georgia father's advice for 
dealing with
people like this.

about anything they did or said before."

-Paul
```

```
You are entirely correct that I disagree, I provided several reasons
why I disagree. I didn't ask for 'more facts' as you had not provided
any facts at all, merely unsupported assertions.

For example you claimed: "a vast majority of the roofs in
developed countries are dark". That is not a 'fact' and cannot be
because 'dark' is subjective.

Any casual glance with, say, Google Earth on any city will show roofs
that are from bright metallic to black tar, but there is no 'vast
majority' that are 'dark' (except at night).

But even if there were, and they were all painted white you would
still have quite some way to go to show that this would be a
"significant" change, rather than some small fraction of 0.1%.

And even if you could show that the albedo changed, some the effect on
warming would be lost by a similar reduction in radiation at night.
```

```
On 2009-12-14 09:56:07 -0600, Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net> said:

> On Sat, 12 Dec 2009 12:57:23 -0800 (PST), Richard
> <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> tested.

Uh, no. The idea of using light colored roofs, and buildings, has been 
pretty well
accepted as a given in urban planning since at least the 1970's. Thin 
of :dark: as
a heat sink - and there is serious consideration given to keeping an 
urban center
cooler. Indeed, it is said to reference back to Greek or Roman times, 
though I do not
know if that is true or not.

The subject includes "green" roofs (i.e. planted spaces) and also 
things like paving materials,
development patterns, and even how many tress should be planted in an area.

None of this is guess work, though I admit, the Global Warming 
advocates tend to snear
at it or try and dismiss it. I have no earthly idea why.

The math to calculate this is time consuming, but not over complicated. 
People pay
me to run the calculations for them, which is why I recently had to get 
into Fortran
programming - a language I detest but is far easier to use for this than C or
COBOL. (BCD does have some advantages though! )

I asked one of my clients what would be a good reference to suggest, 
and he said this
book - though he cautions that it is two or three years old.

The book has the math you need in it to determine if it is an effective 
strategy or
not.


http://www.wiley.com/WileyCDA/WileyTitle/productCd-0471475815.html


-Paul
```

```
On Dec 15, 4:36 pm, Paul <paul-nospamatall.rauler...@mac.com> wrote:
> On 2009-12-14 09:56:07 -0600, Howard Brazee <how...@brazee.net> said:
>
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> know if that is true or not.

There is no question about whether a high reflective roof is useful in
keeping the house cooler and this may even extend to a wider area. I
look out from here and see many light coloured or metallic painted
roofs exactly for this purpose.

That was not the issue. The claim that you made that this would be
*significant* in the context of global warming.

Further you had claimed that "the vast majority" were "dark", thus
presumably painting them white would make a great difference. As any
casual glance with Google Earth will show there is no "vast majority"
of "dark" for any normal meaning of these terms.

While increasing the reflection of sun light may well reduce the
absorbing of heat by the house during the hours that the sun is high
(and thus will keep the house cool) the emissivity of this may well be
less and radiation is emitted 24 hours a day. Lower emissivity is a
good thing for the house because it reduces heat loss at night,
however it doesn't reduce global warming.

It would be overly simplistic to even claim that white roofs would,
overall, help global warming to _any_ extent, let alone be
"significant".

In any case it seems that most are already light coloured where this
would be useful.

You conflate 'urban planning' and 'global warming' and seem to think
they are the same thing.


> The subject includes "green" roofs (i.e. planted spaces) and also
> things like paving materials,
> development patterns, and even how many tress should be planted in an area.
>

While urban planning may well include those things, your claims did
not.


> None of this is guess work, though I admit, the Global Warming
> advocates tend to snear
> at it or try and dismiss it. I have no earthly idea why.
>

Your original was a snearing dismissal of CO2 with:

"""It is amusing to hear CO2 being blamed, when plain old water vapor
is much much more of an issue.
And a significant change in the planetary albedo could be accomplished
simply by painting roofs white."""


> The math to calculate this is time consuming, but not over complicated.

But, as you said, you don't have the figgering resources.


> People pay
> me to run the calculations for them, which is why I recently had to get
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> -Paul
```

```
On 2009-12-14 23:01:35 -0600, Richard <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> said:

(*sigh*)

Just so y'all know, I really did listen to Doc, so I will restate what 
I did say.

> It is amusing to hear CO2 being blamed, when plain old water vapor
> is much much more of an issue. And a significant change in the 
> planetary albedo could be accomplished
> simply by painting roofs white.


And I stand by that statement. It is true, and anyone can calculate it 
for themselves.  What is a significant change?
*That* is arguable, and if someone wants to discuss that, then it is 
time to start throwing around figures. And that
would be a FUN discussion with this crowd! Everyone would walk away 
having learned something new, or at least
gained some insight from each other.

Apparently, however,  it sets off some people to have their cherished 
causes, like "global warming"
challenged. Or whatever. I don't care and will not respond to such 
ranting any longer.

For the record, there is no doubt the planet is warming up. However, 
the planet has been much
warmer in the past - before human activity could have caused any large 
scale changes in the
planetary patterns. They grew grapes in *Greenland*.



-Paul
```

- **References:** `<ihnqh51geq87k0fuooqje9vkfimqui3ct3@4ax.com> <4b1f4ac7$0$5318$bbae4d71@news.suddenlink.net> <266587d7-3804-4c55-a6a1-2b0a53e4e3f4@b36g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <4b2091a5$0$5322$bbae4d71@news.suddenlink.net> <40893b3c-a973-430b-b983-e4fafd2d18fa@a10g2000pre.googlegroups.com> <stg2i5tf54j4mobdhq2iqpdounuu1v9ogs@4ax.com> <1c6207e5-519a-4248-a575-d67f7b324e40@h40g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <4b21de48$0$5357$bbae4d71@news.suddenlink.net> <hfthdb$a1a$1@reader1.panix.com> <4b23f8fe$0$5363$bbae4d71@news.suddenlink.net> <8c5f78d1-cf4f-48e3-94a8-063d7c9d87e2@b36g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <vtnci5p64qtpda1eip97mp2180n2u9kiuo@4ax.com> <4b270448$0$5346$bbae4d71@news.suddenlink.net> <eca55e28-e1cf-413a-aa37-15779aa88f06@x25g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <4b284438$0$5338$bbae4d71@news.suddenlink.net>`

```
On Dec 16, 3:21 pm, Paul <paul-nospamatall.rauler...@mac.com> wrote:
> On 2009-12-14 23:01:35 -0600, Richard <rip...@Azonic.co.nz> said:
>
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> time to start throwing around figures.

I had already 'threw around some figures', ones that you ignored. The
area of roofs in New Zealand is around 0.1% of the land area. Given
that water vapour, in the form of clouds, could make an actual
significant change in planetary albedo by covering 100% of the land
then 1/1000 of that does not come close.

And that, of course, does not consider that many, if not most, are
already bright so 'white' would make no difference at all to.

The heat balance of the Earth is not just considering the heat that
arrives from the Sun, all that heat must be lost by emission out into
space. Actually a bit more must be lost to cater for heat arriving
from inside the Earth. If less heat is emitted than arrives then the
temperature rises.

While you have indicated 'urban studies' these have a rather different
focus than any needs to reduce global warming. For example in hotter
climates it is quite common for temperatures to drop during the night,
in desert areas sometimes to almost freezing. The needs of houses in
these areas is to reduce the absorbing of heat from the Sun, but it is
also to _reduce_ the emission of heat at night.

The problem is that while white (or better still metallic aluminum)
may well reduce absorbing heat it also reduces emissions. Good for
houses, neutral for global warming.

Of course in colder climates, such as the northern states, canada,
most of Europe, russia and northern china the requirement is to
increase absorbing as well as reducing emission.

As long as you concentrate on the wrong questions (urban planning)
then you will get the wrong answers.


> And that
> would be a FUN discussion with this crowd! Everyone would walk away
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> ranting any longer.

Given your history of personal insults this seems rather disingenuous.

> For the record, there is no doubt the planet is warming up. However,
> the planet has been much
> warmer in the past - before human activity could have caused any large
> scale changes in the
> planetary patterns. They grew grapes in *Greenland*.

The claim that 'they grew grapes in Greenland' is completely false.
This myth is usually based on the term 'vinland' (sometimes vineland)
that some believe was applied to Greenland and/or Newfoundland. The
Vikings did not speak french (except for the Normans of course) where
vin = grape wine. Vinland actually means pasture land, or treeless
grassland.

You are such a mine of misinformation.



> -Paul
>
…[96 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

```
On Dec 17, 2:13 pm, Paul <paul-nospamatall.rauler...@mac.com> wrote:
> On 2009-12-15 22:11:08 -0600, Richard <rip...@Azonic.co.nz> said:
>
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…

Actually, the 'lunatic fringe nonsense' is all from you.

> While
> there is some
> dispute about growing grapes in Greenland, there is no dispute at all
> that one could have
> during the Climatic Optimum.

First of all we are not talking about the "Climatic Optimum",
Greenland was settled in the "Medieval  Optimum". Secondly there is no
dispute that they could _NOT_ grow grapes at that time in Greenland.


> Indeed, there is evidence, though not
> enough to say with absolute
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> most likely were not. )

I think that you made that up. There are plenty of references to wheat
grown in Greenland while grapes were grown in Yorkshire. You probably
confused these.

If you want a description of how hash it was in those settlements I
refer you to "The Quest for America" by Ashe which covers the Vikings
travels and settlements in Greenland and Newfoundland.


> Inconvenient facts for some I am afraid.
>
> To start learning about the Climatic Optimum, try the AMS Glossary.http://amsglossary.allenpress.com/glossary/search?id=climatic-optimum1
>

Unfortunately, although you are making an effort to post links, you
are still a novice at it. Your link is to:

"""Climatic Optimum—An interval of higher summer temperatures (2°–4°C
higher) that followed the retreat of the last Pleistocene glaciers in
North America and Europe, varying in age with location but often
between approximately 5000 and 7000 years ago."""

Viking winemaking equipment from the post-Pleistocene? I don't think
so.

If you are so casual about the links that you post, you didn't even
read this one, then how can anyone trust anything that you say.
```

```
On Mon, 14 Dec 2009 21:36:40 -0600, Paul
<paul-nospamatall.raulerson@mac.com> wrote:

>> It was a guess not backed up with a serious study.   But dark isn't
>> that subjective.   Terms can be defined so that his statement can be
>> tested.
>
>Uh, no. 

The main gist of my post is that dark isn't entirely subjective, and
that this can be tested.    Your first sentence disagrees with my
post, which I doubt is what you meant.

>The idea of using light colored roofs, and buildings, has been 
>pretty well
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>know if that is true or not.

There are lots of things that have been pretty well accepted - which
haven't been tested.   I happen to agree with the logic here, but I am
driving a white car which I inherited from my father-in-law who lived
in the desert.   I don't notice it being cooler because of its color -
maybe it is, maybe it isn't significantly cooler.

But whether something makes sense or not - or whether its significant
- or whether other variables overshadow the theory, can only be
determined by actual testing or measuring.    My common sense can be
fooled - it won't be the first time.

At least with science, the resistance against actually testing is much
smaller than it is in political threads (capitalism vs socialism,
etc), and the desire to use the No True Scotsman argument is much less
as well.
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<hftqa4$hon$2@reader1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<4b1f24f0$0$5340$bbae4d71@news.suddenlink.net> <stg2i5tf54j4mobdhq2iqpdounuu1v9ogs@4ax.com> <1c6207e5-519a-4248-a575-d67f7b324e40@h40g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <kno4i5dd23f0sv98tdt7qn6i8lh1rutl39@4ax.com>`

```
In article <kno4i5dd23f0sv98tdt7qn6i8lh1rutl39@4ax.com>,
Howard Brazee  <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
>On Thu, 10 Dec 2009 11:48:07 -0800 (PST), Richard
><riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>something to measure.

Ow... I am not an engineer nor do I play one on television, Mr Brazee, but 
I'd be interested in seeing comparative maintenance-costs for two 
similarly-located and similarly-trafficed roads, one tarmac (the Latest 
Style, with bits of recycled tires (for chewiness) and fragments of glass 
(for crunchiness) and the other of similarly-Latest-Model concrete.  The 
location should be in a truly 'temperate', ie four well-defined seasons 
and sufficient cold to create a 'hard freeze' (so that salt treatment is 
required).

As I understand concrete - and I do not have much solid understanding of 
it but I've dealt with Concrete Abstracts - there's a condition called 
'spalling' which needs to be taken into account.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Climate Change

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** Richard <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>

- **References:** `<4b1f24f0$0$5340$bbae4d71@news.suddenlink.net> <stg2i5tf54j4mobdhq2iqpdounuu1v9ogs@4ax.com> <1c6207e5-519a-4248-a575-d67f7b324e40@h40g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <kno4i5dd23f0sv98tdt7qn6i8lh1rutl39@4ax.com> <hftqa4$hon$2@reader1.panix.com>`

```
On Dec 12, 5:00 am, docdw...@panix.com () wrote:
> In article <kno4i5dd23f0sv98tdt7qn6i8lh1rut...@4ax.com>,
> Howard Brazee  <how...@brazee.net> wrote:
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> 'spalling' which needs to be taken into account.

We have some concrete suburban roads here, mainly laid in the 1930s to
1950s. One problem with them is that they are inflexible compared to
tarmac and thus tend to crack, this leads to rain seeping through to
the roadbed which then erodes.

Of course we don't have winters here, freezing and salting would be
further significantly detrimental.
```

```
In article <433cce93-e340-4aba-ab21-a2dea7938925@y10g2000prg.googlegroups.com>,
Richard  <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:
>On Dec 12, 5:00?am, docdw...@panix.com () wrote:
>> In article <kno4i5dd23f0sv98tdt7qn6i8lh1rut...@4ax.com>,
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
>the roadbed which then erodes.

I recall seeing some bits of concrete roadway... someplace and thinking 
'Now what happens when the roads are empty, overnight, and then daytime 
traffic/sunlight heats the surface faster than the underside, where the 
dirt is acting as a resevoir of cold, and the ribbon of concrete begins to 
flex like the bimetallic spring in a thermostat?'

>
>Of course we don't have winters here, freezing and salting would be
>further significantly detrimental.

That makes for another bit to consider... in the USA there are strips of 
continuous highway a few thousand kilometers/miles long; it would be 
interesting to listen to the road-engineers 'discussing' where the 
concrete might end/begin and the tarmac might begin/end.

(the '' around 'discussing' is for those who have never heard 
road-engineers disagreeing, in a polite and genteel manner (perhaps with a 
bit of ethanol as lubricant), a possibility which cannot be resolved by a 
handy textbook... or which can, for that matter, eg 'You call that a 
textbook?  They proposed that for *my* group and we laughed it out of the 
classroom, now you want a *real* textbook you have to use...' or 'I don't 
care what the book says, I was workin' on a slab of road in (suitably 
exotic place), where you had to shake out your (garment) every morning 
so's not to get bit by the (wildlife), and let me tell you, it don't 
matter *what* the book says, I *saw*...')

DD
```

```
used tires.  It could be California.  But the idea seems to be that
this is a good way to recycle the millions of tons of used tires lying

program would expand to other places if it proves worthwhile.
```

```
... the band is playing somewhere, and somewhere hearts are light,

>But the idea seems to be that
>this is a good way to recycle the millions of tons of used tires lying
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…

I knew a fellow who, long ago, in Law School stunned his classmates (and 
amused the professor, who had seen similarly) by saying that if a 
Corporation does a cost/benefit analysis of a situation and determines 
that the fine for polluting is US$n/day and the cost for not-polluting is 
US$m/day (where (m) is considered to be significantly less than (n) and 
the intangible benefits considered equal) then it will continue to 
pollute.

DD
```

```
doing this.  Of course the paving industry opposed it.

Jane Pauley did for NBC's Dateline program:

Here's some more recent information :
```

```
SkippyPB  <swiegand@Nospam.neo.rr.com> wrote:
>On Tue, 15 Dec 2009 01:18:23 +0000 (UTC), docdwarf@panix.com () wrote:

>>In article <3f2di558m5951b5ma9jtej48ebvikklh39@4ax.com>,

[snip]

>>>Somewhere in these here United States, someone is making asphalt from
>>>used tires.  It could be California.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>>matter where it is, it sure isn't Mudville.

[snip]

>The used tire thing (now that I've had a little time to research it)
>is pretty old - goes back to 1998.  And it was California that started
>doing this.  Of course the paving industry opposed it.

I recall a fashion/fad/experiment on the East Coast of mixing finely-diced 
glass into asphalt a few years back... but a quick bit of web-searching 
hasn't told me if it is still au courant.  Observations which might be 
broadly applied come from 
<http://www.earthmagazine.org/earth/article/221-7d9-5-16> :

--begin quoted text:

To recycle more garbage, "people have been dumping all sorts of stuff in 
asphalt", says Rajib Mallick, an associate professor of civil and 
environmental engineering at Worcester Polytechnic Institute in 
Massachusetts  old tires, ground-up toilets, glass and shingles, to name a 
few. It can be a good way to reuse unwanted junk, Mallick says, if it 
improves the asphalt and is environmentally safe. 

[snip]

And if there are not enough discarded circuit boards out there, then it 
wont be economically viable to set up the necessary factories to recycle 
them for use in asphalt, he says  which is a problem with many of the 
materials people have tried to add to asphalt, like certain types of 
glass.

--end quoted text

(during the search which yielded the above I came across 
<http://www.dep.state.fl.us/waste/quick_topics/publications/shw/recycling/InnovativeGrants/IGYear10/fullprop/HighlandsCountyAsphaltProposal.pdf> 
and what I found interesting is that the Project Abstract came with the 
warning of 'No more than 20 lines.  Every word over 20 lines will count as 
a one point deduction.')

DD
```

```
On Dec 14, 2:29 am, Alistair <alist...@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
> On Dec 11, 3:20 pm, Howard Brazee <how...@brazee.net> wrote:
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> Doesn't the curing of concrete create CO2? A little self defeating

The other way around. The production of portland cement produces great
quantities of CO2 both because of the heating involved and the removal
of CO2 from the limestone and clay.

During curing it does reabsorb CO2.
```

```
On Dec 12, 4:20 am, Howard Brazee <how...@brazee.net> wrote:
> On Thu, 10 Dec 2009 11:48:07 -0800 (PST), Richard
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> something to measure.

Apart from the structural problems anything which reduces tire
adhesion or interferes with visibility would make roads less useful.

Having a road with a high reflectivity would lead to more accidents
caused by sun-strike or even headlight dazzling. Road markings would
be less visible.

The concrete roads here are probably not much more reflective than the
tarmac, they are grimey with tire marks, oil, and general wear.
```

```
On Thu, 10 Dec 2009 11:48:07 -0800 (PST), Richard
<riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>On Dec 11, 8:01ï¿½am, SkippyPB <swieg...@Nospam.neo.rr.com> wrote:
>> On Thu, 10 Dec 2009 09:14:59 -0800 (PST), Richard
…[148 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Phoenix, Arizona a city I lived in for over 5 years has similar roof
lines.  Many, many houses there have a red tile roof.  The tile being
made from clay.  It is very effective at reflecting the hot desert sun
during the day and holding the heat during the night.  I lived in a
house that had such a roof and it used a swamp cooler rather than air
conditioning to cool the house.  True, it wasn't as cool during the
day as an air conditioned house, but it was livable and a heck of a
lot cheaper.


>> The real discussion shouldn't be whether this is done nor not but
>> whether it is as effective as implied. ï¿½My own feeling is yes it
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>> Steve

Regards,
```

## Thread: http://realwebwonders.com/ — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Web, XML, modern integration`](topics.md#web)_

### http://realwebwonders.com/

- **From:** ashok <bram02234@gmail.com>
- **Date:** 2009-12-07T21:34:56-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<539493fc-abe0-4d6e-abee-d300dc9b390d@z4g2000prh.googlegroups.com>`

```


One of the most important aspects of a website today is the webcopy -
sometimes called the sales letter.

In days gone bye, banners and graphics were all important but now it's
the words that do the selling.

This short article will give you some pointers about writing good
webcopy.
http://realwebwonders.com/
```

## Thread: Costa Rica’s Real Estate — 1 msgs

### Costa Rica’s Real Estate

- **From:** Bret G Dudl <costaricaviews2009@gmail.com>
- **Date:** 2009-12-08T02:45:38-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bf780a29-22e5-421b-81a8-8ba79759bfd0@o9g2000prg.googlegroups.com>`

```

I am running the blog http://www.costaricaviews.com/.

I have put there Costa Rica’s Real Estate Development Climate. I put
here for
your information.
Currently, in the Central and South Pacific regions of Costa Rica,
private development has funded three marinas and a dozen major coastal
developments worth nearly $2 Billion. Marina Pez Vela in Quepos will
open 100 slips next year, the Golfito 217 slip Marina is almost
finished with their Environmental permitting process, and the ultimate
Carara bay Marina & Resort in Punta Leon has broken ground on the
country’s most ambitious marina development ever.  Furthermore, the
Green Coast Pacific development in Dominical is underway with several
of their 12 developments already completed totaling $650 million in
Dominical.

Tourism has become Costa Rica’s leading industry. The government has
encouraged environmentally responsible development. The country is
ranked #1 in the world for eco-tourism.  Significant development began
in the north Pacific region, when the government began operation of
the international airport in Liberia in 1995. Prior to the global real
estate downturn, property values in the north jumped in some locales
by 2000% in the last 10 years (3000% near the new airport) as
vacationers and vacation home buyers had easy access to air
transportation.

With a new golf course, many restaurants, retail and rooftops coming
out of the ground, the southern pacific zone in Costa Rica very well
may be the cure to any unhealthy real estate portfolio.

You may get more details at:-
http://www.costaricaviews.com/costa-ricas-real-estate-development-climate/
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: License Management Service

- **From:** "me" <me@home.com>
- **Date:** 2009-12-17T11:48:58
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<872dnanAtccxh7fWnZ2dnUVZ8sCdnZ2d@bt.com>`
- **References:** `<4b22bfee$0$276$14726298@news.sunsite.dk> <5e16dbd6-8e04-4c85-8471-d886d750169c@p8g2000yqb.googlegroups.com> <4b294455$0$273$14726298@news.sunsite.dk>`

```
Article 1594 from the MF Knowledgebase - try their Resolution.

Unable to connect to License Manager
This article explains what to do if the message "Unable to connect to 
License Manager" occurs.

Problem:
When running APPTRACK we encountered the error "Unable to connect to License 
Manager". Does this type of error only occur in the Windows runtime product 
server for COBOL version 5.0 WrapPack 5 onwards?

Resolution:
License Manager runs as a Windows Service. First, check that the Micro Focus 
License Manager has been started. Run the services menu (services.msc) or 
access it via the control panel.  Search for Micro Focus License Manager. If 
it is present, hit the Start option. If not present, from a DOS prompt, 
enter the command mflmwin -i which will install the service and should also 
start it. If it doesn't start automatically, then start it using the 
Services menu.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: License Management Service

```
Micro focus Net Express Personal Edition does not have any program

any license manager service in my services.

Thanks
```

## Thread: AIX COBOL & Shell Script & AIX commands — 14 msgs

### AIX COBOL & Shell Script & AIX commands

- **From:** gaya3 <gayathri.kalyanasundaram@wipro.com>
- **Date:** 2009-12-15T04:09:03-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<42898ffb-c829-489a-b366-18aec7e0d436@1g2000vbm.googlegroups.com>`

```
Hi,

Is there any way to call a AIX shell script from IBM AIX COBOL? Could
someone give me the link to the syntax/examples? Also, Is there a way
to call system commands like "Echo 123" etc. using COBOL.

It would be very helpful for me if syntax/examples are provided.

Thanks!
gaya3
```

```
Nopes.. This is not any kind of assignment.. I know that we can invoke

code. How to do this? One more thing, I did search a lot for the
solution and am coming to this forum as the last resort. Also, I tried
using CALL "system" ... The said command is applicable only for MF
cobol and not for IBM AIX cobol. Please correct if wrong and also,
please let me know how to call system commands from cobol code. It
must be possible to call shell scripts from cobol. I just want to know
how it is done!
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: AIX COBOL & Shell Script & AIX commands

- **From:** Richard <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2009-12-15T20:23:11-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4121b095-1b35-4b1a-a32a-4ba7e0e24f9e@2g2000prl.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<42898ffb-c829-489a-b366-18aec7e0d436@1g2000vbm.googlegroups.com> <85a3071d-3bc6-419f-9a44-09f06bfcd3b4@w19g2000pre.googlegroups.com> <136f45a4-b460-4c19-ab4c-8ebec9891c6e@h10g2000vbm.googlegroups.com>`

```
On Dec 16, 4:25 pm, gaya3 <gayathri.kalyanasunda...@wipro.com> wrote:
> On Dec 15, 12:29 pm, Richard <rip...@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:
>
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…
> how it is done!

You asked how to "call a AIX shell script". The way this is done in C
is via a "system(scriptcommand);" subroutine call.

system() is a standard C library routine. The special MF call is CALL
"SYSTEM". Note that this is upper case. The lower case version CALL
"system" should use the standard C library.

That you find it does not work could be caused by one of several
reasons, such as it is trying to do a dynamic call, or has been
remapped to upper-case, or you haven't supplied the C library. If you
can call C then you should be able to call C's library routines.
```

```
Thank you for the reply!
I have posted the code that i tried and also the error.
========================================================
      *
       Identification Division.
       Program-Id. SysCmds.

       Data Division.
       Working-storage Section.
       01  cmd-line   pic x(3)  value "sh".
       01  cmd-line2  pic x(30) value "cp f1 f2 >errfile 2>&1".

       procedure division.
           display "Test"
           call "SYSTEM" using cmd-line
           display "Back in program"
           call "SYSTEM" using cmd-line2
           display "Back in program"
           stop run.
==============================================================

Error during the execution of the code:
~> cob2 try1.cbl
IBM COBOL for AIX  2.0.0 in progress ...
End of compilation 1,  program SYSCMDS,  no statements flagged.
ld: 0711-317 ERROR: Undefined symbol: .SYSTEM

=============================================================

As i understand from the above code, the IBM AIX cobol doesnt use
SYSTEM command. Is there any other possibility. Or, Can the scripts be
called only from C/C++ programs! I dont think the latter would be the
case. There should be some way to call scripts directly from ibm aix
cobol when there is a way in MF cobol!!!Am i doing something wrong
here?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: AIX COBOL & Shell Script & AIX commands

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Louis Krupp <lkrupp_nospam@indra.com.invalid>
- **Date:** 2009-12-16T09:34:19-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<jcKdnW21E6qQkbTWnZ2dnUVZ_tqdnZ2d@indra.net>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<84e4937c-26ca-4e5c-8b20-6f2f4c6e6059@20g2000vbz.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<42898ffb-c829-489a-b366-18aec7e0d436@1g2000vbm.googlegroups.com> <85a3071d-3bc6-419f-9a44-09f06bfcd3b4@w19g2000pre.googlegroups.com> <136f45a4-b460-4c19-ab4c-8ebec9891c6e@h10g2000vbm.googlegroups.com> <4121b095-1b35-4b1a-a32a-4ba7e0e24f9e@2g2000prl.googlegroups.com> <84e4937c-26ca-4e5c-8b20-6f2f4c6e6059@20g2000vbz.googlegroups.com>`

```
gaya3 wrote:
<snip>
> As i understand from the above code, the IBM AIX cobol doesnt use
> SYSTEM command. Is there any other possibility. Or, Can the scripts be
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> here?

Yes.  You're assuming that because you would like AIX COBOL to have 
"call system" or its equivalent, it must have one.  It's OK;  wishful 
thinking is human nature.

I looked at the AIX COBOL manual online (as I assume you did), and I 
couldn't find anything.  There are examples of AIX COBOL calling C 
functions, and at this point, I think you should probably do that.

Louis
```

```
Pete.
```

- **References:** `<42898ffb-c829-489a-b366-18aec7e0d436@1g2000vbm.googlegroups.com> <85a3071d-3bc6-419f-9a44-09f06bfcd3b4@w19g2000pre.googlegroups.com> <136f45a4-b460-4c19-ab4c-8ebec9891c6e@h10g2000vbm.googlegroups.com> <4121b095-1b35-4b1a-a32a-4ba7e0e24f9e@2g2000prl.googlegroups.com> <84e4937c-26ca-4e5c-8b20-6f2f4c6e6059@20g2000vbz.googlegroups.com>`

```
On Dec 16, 3:10 am, gaya3 <gayathri.kalyanasunda...@wipro.com> wrote:
> On Dec 15, 11:23 pm, Richard <rip...@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:
>
…[127 more quoted lines elided]…
> Error during the execution of the code:

> ~> cob2 try1.cbl
> IBM COBOL for AIX  2.0.0 in progress ...
> End of compilation 1,  program SYSCMDS,  no statements flagged.
> ld: 0711-317 ERROR: Undefined symbol: .SYSTEM
>

NO NO NO! This is not a run time error. You compiled the code but it
failed to link. ld is the system linker. As the error message
says .SYSTEM is an undefined symbol. That means one of several things.

1. Perhaps you should have tried CALL "system" instead.
2. I suspect that the Cobol compiler upper cases symbols unless you
insert a compiler directive for mixed case. See the Programmer's
manual.
3. You may have to include the name of the program library that
contains the C routine on your command line.

[see any *nix text for details]

> =============================================================
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> here?

NO. Just because vendor A does something does not mean that vendor B
has to do it as well (unless perhaps A is IBM .... smile). Shelling
out from a Cobol program is not mandated by any Cobol language
standard. It is not portable. You get what you get.

Based on what you have posted I suggest that you look for alternatives
in the realm of shell scripting before you attempt either mixed
language programming or writing a C/C++ wrapper around the system
routine.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: AIX COBOL & Shell Script & AIX commands

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Richard <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2009-12-16T09:38:08-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cb8e253f-6c36-45d1-a35f-3a5cb5c7700a@f18g2000prf.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<42898ffb-c829-489a-b366-18aec7e0d436@1g2000vbm.googlegroups.com> <85a3071d-3bc6-419f-9a44-09f06bfcd3b4@w19g2000pre.googlegroups.com> <136f45a4-b460-4c19-ab4c-8ebec9891c6e@h10g2000vbm.googlegroups.com> <4121b095-1b35-4b1a-a32a-4ba7e0e24f9e@2g2000prl.googlegroups.com> <84e4937c-26ca-4e5c-8b20-6f2f4c6e6059@20g2000vbz.googlegroups.com>`

```
On Dec 16, 9:10 pm, gaya3 <gayathri.kalyanasunda...@wipro.com> wrote:
> On Dec 15, 11:23 pm, Richard <rip...@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:
>
…[128 more quoted lines elided]…
>


As already pointed out the 'ld' linker command could not find 'SYSTEM'
in the list of libraries that it searched.

I have already told you that the correct name for the C library
routine is "system" in lower case.

You will also need to null terminate the parameter to the call. I had
indicated this with '& x"00"'. You may need to use LOW-VALUES or some
other to get a null into the string, such as:
     MOVE LOW-VALUE TO cmd-line(3:1)



> =============================================================
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> here?

Yes. You are not listening.
```

#### ↳ Re: AIX COBOL & Shell Script & AIX commands

- **From:** e p chandler <epc8@juno.com>
- **Date:** 2009-12-15T10:52:48-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<53ec2187-8cd0-49ad-819d-eb13c2c53e9a@26g2000yqo.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<42898ffb-c829-489a-b366-18aec7e0d436@1g2000vbm.googlegroups.com>`

```
On Dec 15, 7:09 am, gaya3 <gayathri.kalyanasunda...@wipro.com> wrote:
> Hi,
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> gaya3

The first question should not be HOW but WHY? (Unless you are porting
from MicroFocus).

Why go out of process to run a shell script when you more easily could
do whatever you want to do within a shell script first? If needed,
redirect output to a file, then read the file from Cobol.

See http://www-01.ibm.com/software/awdtools/cobol/aix/library/

Read the section of Chapter 26 on calling C/C++ programs. Be prepared
to shoot yourself in the foot .... multiple times.
```

---

<a id="t-382d520035"></a>
## Thread: Cobol vs. Images — 4 msgs

### Cobol vs. Images

- **From:** Paul <paul-nospamatall.raulerson@mac.com>
- **Date:** 2009-12-21T22:08:35-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4b304642$0$5329$bbae4d71@news.suddenlink.net>`

```
Here is an actual COBOL question. :)

I'm working on porting an Document Image Management system to COBOL 
using PerCOBOL.  I am finding I really *really* like this compiler a 
lot, and the more I study it, the more I get impressed. Indeed, it even 
has rudimentary image management code in it, which allows me to display 
an image
on a GUI screen.  I like I can package up the JAR file and everything 
runs just fine under Windows, zLinux, MacOS, and Linux. :)

But I need to do some work on the image as it comes in - scale it, 
resize it, etc.  So, given that I do not know all that much about image 
manipulaiton,
is there a COBOL based image manipulation library anyone would care to 
recommend?

Or a better method of dealing with this issue?

PerCOBOL generates to Java, so I guess I could use a Java library, but 
what I really want is something simpler. :)

Just fishing for ideas folks.
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol vs. Images

- **From:** Richard <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2009-12-21T22:33:59-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
>
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> Just fishing for ideas folks.

ImageMagick

http://www.imagemagick.org/
```

## Thread: OT: Happy holidays everyone! — 12 msgs

_Topics: [`Off-topic and spam`](topics.md#spam)_

### OT: Happy holidays everyone!

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2009-12-23T23:38:28+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7pea93FhooU1@mid.individual.net>`

```
I don't know about you folks, but it seems amazing to me that we are into 
2010 in just over a week...

Anyway, I just wanted to wish all of you a peaceful, enjoyable, and happy 
holiday season, with good health and prosperity in the coming year.

Personally, I'm taking a few days off, although I'm not going away. I plan 
to relax and enjoy the sunshine, the beaches, the food and drink, and my 
friends...

I have a new project for the New Year and it promises to be interesting, but 
I'll pick it up when I've had a break and am fresh...:-)

Best to All,

Pete.
```

```
and chilling out and sleeping in every morning.  I love that.

all have Happy Holidays as well.

Cheers,
```

<a id="t-e55baaf2b9"></a>
## Thread: Reminder: Computer Programs are not Reality — 2 msgs

### Reminder: Computer Programs are not Reality

- **From:** "HeyBub" <heybub@NOSPAMgmail.com>
- **Date:** 2009-12-26T06:45:00-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<etCdnZ5E06TQmKvWnZ2dnUVZ_rednZ2d@earthlink.com>`

```
"The present climate debate is a classic conflict between data and computer 
programs. The computer programs are the source of concern over climate 
change and global warming, not the data. Data are measurements. Computer 
programs are artificial constructs."

http://www.powerlineblog.com/archives/2009/12/025239.php

It's true. Every time I sit down at World of Warcraft I think "If I don't 
save the world, who will?"
```

#### ↳ Re: Reminder: Computer Programs are not Reality

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()
- **Date:** 2009-12-26T15:44:36+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<hh5b14$5t5$1@reader1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<etCdnZ5E06TQmKvWnZ2dnUVZ_rednZ2d@earthlink.com>`

```
In article <etCdnZ5E06TQmKvWnZ2dnUVZ_rednZ2d@earthlink.com>,
HeyBub <heybub@NOSPAMgmail.com> wrote:
>"The present climate debate is a classic conflict between data and computer 
>programs. The computer programs are the source of concern over climate 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>save the world, who will?" 

This might be precluded by thinking 'If I don't sit down to play a 
computer game what might be lost?'

DD
```

<a id="t-429453a420"></a>
## Thread: Converting COBOL print file to HTML help — 13 this month / 21 total

_Topics: [`Migration and conversion`](topics.md#migration) · [`Web, XML, modern integration`](topics.md#web)_

### Converting COBOL print file to HTML help

- **From:** razor <iruddock@blueyonder.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2009-12-30T17:27:53-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<0d471743-7377-4445-ab3f-70db4bfdbf59@n38g2000yqf.googlegroups.com>`

```
Hello chaps

I am currently working on a program that creates a number of customers
orders within a 'print file'. A separate file is created per depot/
store. Typically this would contain 200+ orders. They are ordered by
the delivery sequence number. On change of 'route no', the program
throws a new page (WRITE AFTER PAGE).

Now I already have a program that converts a print file to HTML and
emails to a location. We do this simply by inserting the contents into
a very simple HTML file as preformatted text.

My problem is that the store wishes to print the file out and give
each page(s) to each delivery route driver. I need to replace the FF
(x"0C") characters with some other way of throwing a page in HTML, but
within the PRE tag. Or maybe I need to close the PRE tag, throw a page
and reopen. I've tried a few things all to no avail. Not exactly a
COBOL question, but its all about 'HTML for people who mainly only
know COBOL'.

Many thanks for any help with this.

Razor
```

#### ↳ Re: Converting COBOL print file to HTML help

- **From:** Richard <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2009-12-30T18:02:05-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8fd0c21b-a989-4b9a-8bad-7ea7777fbdf5@a32g2000yqm.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<0d471743-7377-4445-ab3f-70db4bfdbf59@n38g2000yqf.googlegroups.com>`

```
On Dec 31, 2:27 pm, razor <irudd...@blueyonder.co.uk> wrote:
> Hello chaps
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> a very simple HTML file as preformatted text.

What is the point of 'converting it to HTML' if what you do is to
email it?

I could see the point of making it HTML if it were to be accessed from
a web site by the client using a browser, but email does not need HTML
at all.

If you emailed it as a text attachment then it could be opened in,
say, notepad or any text editor and printed from there _with_ page
throws.


> My problem is that the store wishes to print the file out and give
> each page(s) to each delivery route driver. I need to replace the FF
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> know COBOL'.

HTML is a _display_ format and not a print format. HTML will normally
ignore any control characters such as CR, LF, and FF, or even multiple
spaces and tabs, as these are irrelevant to the markup tags.

The answer is going to be: Don't use HTML

If I were to do this I would send each print file as a text attachment
to the email (or each as a separate email) with FF embedded and with a
mime type that was linked in the client to a print program (eg lpr on
Unix/Linux).

Then clicking on the attachment will cause it to print (including form
feeds).
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Converting COBOL print file to HTML help

- **From:** razor <iruddock@blueyonder.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2009-12-30T18:52:58-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c93c84d-7830-4f4b-a46c-47081a19b1a8@a21g2000yqc.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<0d471743-7377-4445-ab3f-70db4bfdbf59@n38g2000yqf.googlegroups.com> <8fd0c21b-a989-4b9a-8bad-7ea7777fbdf5@a32g2000yqm.googlegroups.com>`

```
Hello Richard

Excellent points.

'I' don't want to send it via HTML. I was told in the past. Yes we
tried sending text, but the users found it too complicated (I was
told). I have a single 'engine' that takes a print file and emails it
as a HTML file as an attachment. When they open it it opens in a
browser. I don't want to go into this and change it to email either a
text file or a HTML file depending on.....

Don't know what 'mime types' are. Even if it is correct, nobody is
going to pay for my time to find out. Almost embarrassing posting this
as the reason however its 3 in the morning and I haven't emailed the
orders yet that came in at 5pm and need to be at the depot when they
open at about 4pm.

Anyway, I've just had one of those moments where you don't give up and
eventually find what you need to do, thanks to the 'net. My HTML file
went;

<pre>
text line 1
text line 2
text line n
</pre>

Crude, but it worked.

I now have;

<div style="page-break-after: always;"&nbsp</div>

<pre>
text line 1
text line 2
text line 3
</pre>

<div style="page-break-after: always;"&nbsp</div>

<pre>
text line 4
text line 5
text line 6
</pre>

And it works and passes W3 org tests.

Thanks anyway

Razor
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Converting COBOL print file to HTML help

- **From:** Richard <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2009-12-30T21:48:14-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5ba12283-d325-484e-9314-cbaae8f0da5a@l30g2000yqb.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<0d471743-7377-4445-ab3f-70db4bfdbf59@n38g2000yqf.googlegroups.com> <8fd0c21b-a989-4b9a-8bad-7ea7777fbdf5@a32g2000yqm.googlegroups.com> <3c93c84d-7830-4f4b-a46c-47081a19b1a8@a21g2000yqc.googlegroups.com>`

```
On Dec 31, 3:52 pm, razor <irudd...@blueyonder.co.uk> wrote:
> Hello Richard
>
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> open at about 4pm.

Is that supposed to be 4am ?

Are you emailing these manually ?  Good grief, building an email with
attachment and having it sent automatically via a job step or cron job
is^H^H should be easy.

Here is a template for an email with attachment. Note that the tags
are marked with %name% for substitution.

%atype% is the attachment's mime type which could be text/plain or
text/html or even application/something. %name% can also control what
happens to the attachment, so eg name.csv would be opened in
OpenOffice.org or Excell or ...

Note that the boundary mark can be any random squiggle of characters
that you choose. You can add any number of attachments but I usually
have the program send one email per file.


From: %from%
To: %to%
Subject: %subject%
Date: %date%
MIME-Version: 1.0
Content-Type: Multipart/Mixed;
  boundary="Boundary-=_nWlrBbmQBhCDarzOwKkYHIDdqSCD"
MIME-Version: 1.0

--Boundary-=_nWlrBbmQBhCDarzOwKkYHIDdqSCD
Content-Type: text/plain
Content-Transfer-Encoding: 8bit

%message%

--Boundary-=_nWlrBbmQBhCDarzOwKkYHIDdqSCD
Content-Type: %atype%;
    name="%name%"
Content-Disposition: attachment; filename="%name%"
Content-Transfer-Encoding: %encoding%

%attach%
--Boundary-=_nWlrBbmQBhCDarzOwKkYHIDdqSCD


>
> Anyway, I've just had one of those moments where you don't give up and
…[33 more quoted lines elided]…
> Razor
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Converting COBOL print file to HTML help

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** singa <viasilvati71@gmail.com>
- **Date:** 2009-12-31T02:53:55-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<28c16a1c-1e20-4618-b15b-3fa9e2073efc@k17g2000yqh.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<0d471743-7377-4445-ab3f-70db4bfdbf59@n38g2000yqf.googlegroups.com> <8fd0c21b-a989-4b9a-8bad-7ea7777fbdf5@a32g2000yqm.googlegroups.com> <3c93c84d-7830-4f4b-a46c-47081a19b1a8@a21g2000yqc.googlegroups.com> <5ba12283-d325-484e-9314-cbaae8f0da5a@l30g2000yqb.googlegroups.com>`

```
Hy Guys,
i am experiencing with cobol (RmCobol85) since 1984,
all of my reports (from *nix servers) are going to
users or external customers (b.e. invoices) sending
text output from Cobol to email thru a simple PDF
wrapper

In other word the output of cobol ASSING TO PRINT
goes to a pipe, here i have the PDF wrapper, PDF
output file goes to email

At the top of the print output, for each page, i put
the destination email address , if omitted is the
currently logged in user

For each Form Feed the wrapper close the current PDF
and will open the next one.

The PDF wrapper could be a simple PHP script, or some
similar (i am not confident with Php, so i have a C program,
built with lex and Clib)

In this way the attachment is a pure PDF file, with
all the advantages (archiving, printing. virus scan,
portability, ...)

Plus: using PDF is possible to draw box, circle, barcodes, ....

If someone is interested i can send them some samples
of output and more details

Sorry but my English is very bad, and all my software and
manualals are in Italian
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Converting COBOL print file to HTML help

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** razor <iruddock@blueyonder.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2009-12-31T04:46:57-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<182e0eaf-829f-4a43-a7b1-7db429b51167@j5g2000yqm.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<0d471743-7377-4445-ab3f-70db4bfdbf59@n38g2000yqf.googlegroups.com> <8fd0c21b-a989-4b9a-8bad-7ea7777fbdf5@a32g2000yqm.googlegroups.com> <3c93c84d-7830-4f4b-a46c-47081a19b1a8@a21g2000yqc.googlegroups.com> <5ba12283-d325-484e-9314-cbaae8f0da5a@l30g2000yqb.googlegroups.com> <28c16a1c-1e20-4618-b15b-3fa9e2073efc@k17g2000yqh.googlegroups.com>`

```
Hi Singa

Thanks for your suggestion.

I would love to process all of our print files into PDF (who doesn't
these days) but unfortunately the client is still in 1976. Their idea
of 'making things better' is spending millions of £ on a huge very
very well known all encompassing system that was supposed to replace
my invoicing system.

Twice its failed spectacularly because they opt to try and change
their business at the same time as migrating to this new system.

(Going slightly OT now, but maybe some will find interesting)

I sat in a meeting with 5 'company' people and 5 'consultants'
discussing the new pricing rules that would be implemented. The
business proceeded to explain how they currently generate customer
prices in the existing (mine) system. All attempts to demonstrate the
actual process to all simply fell on deaf ears and this was not
because of my own personal skills, they just didn't want to listen.
The consultants had to listen to the business people and implement
what they were telling them. When the trial came around sure enough,
most of the prices were incorrect. I wasn't involved in the trial
because I didn't charge a four figure sum as my daily rate, plus also,
I could be besmirched for 'protecting' the existing system without my
knowledge. I'm a professional and would never have done that.

As I have a family and house to support I shall continue to roll along
with the status quo, although I would relish a new challenge. Now
wheres those golf clubs?

Razor
```

- **References:** `<0d471743-7377-4445-ab3f-70db4bfdbf59@n38g2000yqf.googlegroups.com> <8fd0c21b-a989-4b9a-8bad-7ea7777fbdf5@a32g2000yqm.googlegroups.com> <3c93c84d-7830-4f4b-a46c-47081a19b1a8@a21g2000yqc.googlegroups.com> <5ba12283-d325-484e-9314-cbaae8f0da5a@l30g2000yqb.googlegroups.com>`

```
Hello Richard

Yes, it was supposed to be 4am, sorry. Just got up anyway and it
appears there has not been a problem as my beauty sleep was not
disturbed.

Of course I am not sending any emails 'manually'. COBOL does it in a
loop for each store. The point about the time was, not that I had 200
to do but I didn't yet have the programs installed, orders processed
and subsequently emailed from the server.

I don't run it from a cron job (or equivalent). Users process the
'Process EDI Orders' option when they appear, fix any problems and
then the system emails to locations there and then. Takes about 3
minutes to email I'd estimate.

I've read you (and others) talk about templates before but I've never
made the link on how to use them.

I'm not stupid, but if I've never done something I just don't know
anything about it.

Many thanks for your help Richard, it is appreciated.

Razor
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Converting COBOL print file to HTML help

```
On Wed, 30 Dec 2009 18:52:58 -0800 (PST), razor
<iruddock@blueyonder.co.uk> wrote:

>'I' don't want to send it via HTML. I was told in the past. Yes we
>tried sending text, but the users found it too complicated (I was
>told).

Weird.    Text is "too complicated" and HTML isn't?   In e-mails?

I know some e-mail programs give us options of sending e-mail in plain
text or HTML, and others just send whatever we send.

I wonder what they do with printed text.   Discard it as being too
complicated, I suppose.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Converting COBOL print file to HTML help

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** razor <iruddock@blueyonder.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2009-12-31T09:22:37-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<483280fe-70b0-42d5-aafe-f34dcaa42b0a@u41g2000yqe.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<0d471743-7377-4445-ab3f-70db4bfdbf59@n38g2000yqf.googlegroups.com> <8fd0c21b-a989-4b9a-8bad-7ea7777fbdf5@a32g2000yqm.googlegroups.com> <3c93c84d-7830-4f4b-a46c-47081a19b1a8@a21g2000yqc.googlegroups.com> <i6gpj5dvmnbanu1ub43htsi9qqfo46bkgq@4ax.com>`

```
The 'document' is sent as an attachment. Doesn't really matter to me
whether its text or HTML. Problem at store is that when they open it,
some of these people see it opened into Notepad and have never used
this program. Cue screams for training. Surely this echoes your own
experiences? As soon as you tell these staff members that instead of a
weekly fax they will now get a daily email then they will find any way
to wheedle out of agreeing to it. At least most people now have
experience of a browser rather than a text editor like notepad.

Razor
```

```
If it were just a requirement to print then I would probably bypass
all the use of a GUI and implement a simple program to collect email
(sent to a specific address) and extract the attachment and print it
without any user action.

But then I have these sort of things already automated, such as
collecting emails with .xls attachments and processing these in
various ways.
```

```
> On Wed, 30 Dec 2009 18:52:58 -0800 (PST), razor
>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>

It seems that it is not the 'text' that is 'too complicated' but the
program(s) that the attachment opens in. The users are used to a
browser, know where 'print' is on the menu, etc. A 'text' attachment
would open in notepad, or worse, ask what was to be used and give a
list that the users have no idea about.



> --
> "In no part of the constitution is more wisdom to be found,
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
```
